    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Synthetic-to-anchor ratio effect (excluding no-anchor strategy)
try:
    ANCHOR_SIZE = 200
    ratios = [ns / ANCHOR_SIZE for ns in SYNTH_POOL_SIZES]
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Synthetic Text Dataset — Final Metrics vs Synthetic-to-Anchor Ratio (Anchor Strategies Only)",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    anchor_strategies = ["TADA (tail anchor)", "Random anchor"]
    for mi, (metric, mlabel, myl) in enumerate(
        zip(metrics, metric_labels, metric_ylabels)
    ):
        for si, strat_name in enumerate(anchor_strategies):
            vals = [
                experiment_data[f"n_synth_{ns}"][strat_name][metric][-1]
                for ns in SYNTH_POOL_SIZES
            ]
            axes[mi].plot(
                ratios,
                vals,
                marker=synth_markers[si],
                color=colors[strat_name],
                ls=linestyles[strat_name],
                label=strat_name,
            )
        axes[mi].set_title(mlabel)
        axes[mi].set_xlabel("Synthetic-to-Anchor Ratio (N_SYNTH / ANCHOR_SIZE)")
        axes[mi].set_ylabel(myl)
        axes[mi].legend(fontsize=8)
        axes[mi].grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_synth_anchor_ratio_summary.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: synthetic_text_synth_anchor_ratio_summary.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating ratio plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 5: Training loss curves across generations for each strategy (using N_SYNTH=2000 as representative)
try:
    rep_synth = "n_synth_2000"
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Synthetic Text Dataset — Training Loss Curves per Generation | N_SYNTH=2000",
        fontsize=13,
    )
    for si, strat_name in enumerate(strategies):
        train_losses = experiment_data[rep_synth][strat_name]["train_losses"]
        for gi, ep_losses in enumerate(train_losses):
            axes[si].plot(
                range(1, len(ep_losses) + 1), ep_losses, marker="o", label=f"Gen {gi}"
            )
        axes[si].set_title(f"{strat_name}")
        axes[si].set_xlabel("Epoch")
        axes[si].set_ylabel("Training Loss")
        axes[si].legend(fontsize=7)
        axes[si].grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_training_loss_curves_nsynth2000.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: synthetic_text_training_loss_curves_nsynth2000.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating training loss plot: {e}")
    plt.close()
plot_plan: I need to create visualizations for an experiment comparing three strategies (TADA tail anchor, Random anchor, No anchor) across different synthetic pool sizes (500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000) over multiple generations. The metrics tracked are tail token coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence. I'll create several plots: (1) per-synthetic-pool-size metric curves showing all three strategies, (2) per-strategy comparison across pool sizes, (3) a summary plot of final-generation metrics vs pool size, and (4) a synthetic-to-anchor ratio plot. I'll load the experiment data from the .npy file and plot only real data. Each plot will be in its own try-except block and saved with descriptive names. I'll limit the per-pool-size plots to at most 5 figures (which matches the 5 pool sizes exactly). All figures will have clear titles and labels.

plot_analyses.analysis: Across synthetic pool sizes (N_SYNTH on log scale), tail coverage is near-saturated (~1.0) for TADA and mostly for random anchoring, while the no-anchor condition shows a noticeable dip at an intermediate pool size (coverage drops to ~0.96) despite being ~1.0 elsewhere. This suggests the current tail-coverage metric is either too coarse (ceiling effect) or the tail set is defined such that it’s easy to cover; it is not strongly discriminative except for a single collapse-like event. Perplexity and KL divergence, however, clearly separate conditions at small N_SYNTH: no-anchor has very high PPL (~180+) and KL (~0.47) at the smallest pool size, indicating strong collapse when synthetic data is scarce/low-diversity. As N_SYNTH increases, no-anchor rapidly improves and converges toward anchored strategies (PPL ~120, KL ~0.07), implying that simply enlarging the synthetic pool can partially substitute for real anchoring. Between anchored methods, random anchor tends to have slightly better (lower) PPL and KL than TADA at most pool sizes in this plot, which is contrary to the TADA hypothesis; the differences are small but consistent in PPL. This could indicate (i) tail selection proxy is noisy/misaligned, (ii) anchor size or mixing ratio is already sufficient such that selection doesn’t matter, or (iii) the evaluation set emphasizes head distribution (PPL/KL) more than tail preservation.

The same pattern is reproduced with labels explicitly referencing a synthetic text dataset and metrics evaluated on real data: tail token coverage again saturates near 1.0 for TADA and random, with no-anchor dipping at one setting. PPL on real data and KL from real show strong collapse for no-anchor at the smallest N_SYNTH (PPL ~180+, KL ~0.47), then convergence as N_SYNTH grows. Random anchoring again slightly outperforms TADA in PPL/KL across most N_SYNTH values. This replication suggests the effect is not a plotting artifact but a property of the current experimental configuration/metric definitions. It also suggests that, in this setup, the main driver of collapse is synthetic pool size (diversity) rather than anchor selection strategy, except in the low N_SYNTH regime where any anchoring helps.

When varying synthetic-to-anchor ratio (N_SYNTH/ANCHOR_SIZE), tail coverage is perfectly flat at 1.0 for TADA across all ratios, while random anchor shows dips to ~0.98 at higher ratios (10 and 40) but recovers at ratio 20. This is the clearest evidence in these plots that tail-focused anchoring stabilizes tail coverage under more aggressive synthetic dominance. However, perplexity and KL show a tradeoff: random anchor achieves lower PPL than TADA at low ratios (5–10), while TADA becomes better at higher ratios (20–40), with the best TADA PPL around ratio ~20 and best KL around ratio ~40. This suggests TADA may be more sample-efficient in the regime where real anchors are very scarce (high synthetic-to-anchor ratio), aligning with the paper’s claim, but may not help (and can slightly hurt) when anchors are plentiful. The non-monotonicity (e.g., TADA KL higher at ratio 10 than 5) indicates variance/sensitivity; multiple seeds and confidence intervals are needed.

This figure mirrors the previous ratio sweep but framed as 'anchor strategies only' on the synthetic text dataset. The same qualitative conclusions hold: TADA keeps tail token coverage at 1.0 across ratios, while random has occasional drops (~0.98) at higher ratios. Random is better on PPL at low ratios, while TADA is better at higher ratios (notably around 20–40). KL similarly favors random at low ratios but favors TADA at the highest ratio (40). This strengthens the interpretation that TADA’s advantage is specifically in the low-real-data regime (high ratio), which is exactly the regime of practical interest for anchoring.

Training loss curves per generation at N_SYNTH=2000 show that within each generation, loss decreases with epochs and then plateaus; across generations, later generations generally start and end at lower training loss for all strategies, with the no-anchor condition achieving the lowest final training loss by Gen 5 (~4.23) compared to TADA (~4.41) and random (~4.44). This is a classic warning sign: lower training loss on self-generated/synthetic data does not imply better alignment to the real distribution; it can reflect overfitting to a narrowing synthetic distribution (collapse) that becomes easier to model. Thus, these curves are consistent with collapse dynamics: no-anchor becomes 'easier' to fit over generations even as real-data PPL/KL degrade in other plots (especially at small N_SYNTH). The similarity between TADA and random loss curves suggests anchor selection does not strongly affect optimization difficulty on the mixed training set at this N_SYNTH; differences show up more in distributional metrics.

For the no-anchor strategy, varying N_SYNTH across generations shows severe collapse at N_SYNTH=500: PPL on real data increases dramatically from ~115 to ~183 by Gen 5, and KL rises from ~0.02 to ~0.47. For larger synthetic pools (1000–8000), degradation is much smaller (PPL ends ~120–125; KL ends ~0.06–0.09). Tail token coverage is again mostly saturated at 1.0 with a few dips (notably N_SYNTH=4000 drops to ~0.96 at Gen 5; N_SYNTH=2000/8000 show ~0.98 at Gen 4), which does not track the dramatic PPL/KL collapse at N_SYNTH=500. This mismatch reinforces that the current tail coverage metric is not capturing the collapse observed by PPL/KL, or that collapse here is more about overall distribution shift than literal absence of the predefined tail tokens.

For TADA, across N_SYNTH values, PPL on real data increases with generation for all pool sizes, but the increase is moderate compared to the no-anchor N_SYNTH=500 case (e.g., N_SYNTH=500 ends ~124 vs ~183 without anchors). Larger N_SYNTH generally yields better final PPL (N_SYNTH=8000 ends lowest, ~118–119). KL similarly increases with generation and is lowest for larger pools, with some late-generation jumps (e.g., N_SYNTH=2000 rises sharply by Gen 5). Tail token coverage is mostly 1.0 with a couple dips to ~0.98 around Gen 2–3 for N_SYNTH=2000/4000, suggesting occasional instability but far less than collapse. Overall, TADA clearly prevents catastrophic collapse in the low N_SYNTH regime, but its advantage over random anchoring is not obvious from PPL/KL here; the main effect is 'any anchoring' plus 'bigger synthetic pool'.

For random anchoring, PPL and KL trajectories are broadly similar to TADA: gradual degradation over generations, with larger N_SYNTH yielding lower final PPL/KL. Tail coverage shows more frequent dips than TADA (e.g., N_SYNTH=4000 dips to ~0.96 at Gen 3; N_SYNTH=8000 dips to ~0.98 at Gen 4; N_SYNTH=2000 dips to ~0.98 at Gen 2 and Gen 5). Interestingly, random anchoring often achieves slightly lower PPL than TADA at matched N_SYNTH (consistent with the final-metric plots), while TADA appears more stable on the tail-coverage metric. This suggests a potential head–tail tradeoff: random anchors preserve head distribution (better PPL/KL) while TADA preserves tail tokens (better coverage), but the tail metric needs to be strengthened to make this claim convincing.

At N_SYNTH=500, tail token coverage is completely flat at 1.0 for all strategies, yet the no-anchor condition collapses strongly in PPL and KL over generations, while TADA and random remain much more stable (PPL ends ~124 and ~121 respectively; KL ends ~0.095 and ~0.07 respectively). This is strong evidence that tail coverage as currently computed is not sensitive to the collapse mode that PPL/KL detect. It may be measuring presence/absence of a small set of tokens rather than distributional mass in the tail. For the core hypothesis (tail erosion), you likely need a metric that captures probability mass shift (e.g., tail log-prob, rank-frequency slope, or coverage at multiple rarity thresholds) rather than binary coverage.

At N_SYNTH=8000, all strategies have similar PPL and KL trajectories with only modest degradation; by Gen 5, PPL is ~118.7 (TADA), ~119.3 (random), and ~120.1 (no-anchor), and KL converges around ~0.067–0.070. Tail coverage is again near 1.0 with small dips for random and no-anchor at Gen 4–5 (~0.98). This indicates that with a sufficiently large synthetic pool, collapse is largely mitigated even without anchors, and differences between anchoring strategies become small. This supports an important nuance for the paper: TADA’s value proposition is primarily when synthetic data is limited or expensive to generate (or when synthetic quality is low), not necessarily when you can generate very large pools.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots compare TADA (tail anchors), random anchors, and no-anchor across synthetic pool sizes, synthetic-to-anchor ratios, and generations. Catastrophic collapse appears mainly for no-anchor with small synthetic pools (N_SYNTH=500), visible in sharply rising real-data perplexity and KL divergence. Increasing N_SYNTH substantially reduces collapse even without anchors. TADA’s clearest advantage over random is in maintaining saturated tail-token coverage at high synthetic-to-anchor ratios, while random often has slightly better perplexity/KL in many settings. Tail coverage metric shows ceiling effects and sometimes fails to reflect collapse detected by PPL/KL, suggesting the need for a more sensitive tail-mass metric and multiple-seed error bars.

overall_plan: This research investigates strategies for improving tail token coverage in language model training across multiple generations of data synthesis. The work proceeds in two main phases: (1) Hyperparameter tuning for the learning rate (LR), testing values of 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, and 3e-3 across all strategies to identify the optimal LR based on tail coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence metrics; and (2) An ablation study on Model Reinitialization vs. Fine-tuning Between Generations, which compares three strategies using the TADA (tail anchor) data mixing approach: full model reinitialization (baseline), warm-start fine-tuning with the same learning rate, and warm-start fine-tuning with a decayed learning rate. The ablation study aims to determine whether retaining model weights across generations (warm-starting) is beneficial compared to reinitializing from scratch, and whether learning rate decay further helps in the warm-start setting. All experiments track tail token coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence across generations, with results saved and plotted for comprehensive comparison.

metric_names.description: Tail token coverage of the final generation (Gen 5)
Perplexity evaluated on real data; final value is from the final generation (Gen 5), best value is the lowest across all generations
KL divergence of the model's distribution from the real data distribution; final value is from the final generation (Gen 5), best value is the lowest across all generations
Training loss; final value is from the final generation's final epoch, best value is the lowest across all generations and epochs

import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
import numpy as np
import random
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import Counter

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)

VOCAB_SIZE = 500
SEQ_LEN = 32
N_REAL = 2000
ANCHOR_SIZE = 200
BATCH_SIZE = 64
EPOCHS_PER_GEN = 5
N_GENERATIONS = 5
N_SYNTH = 2000
GEN_SAMPLES = 500
TAIL_FRAC = 0.10
D_MODEL = 128
N_HEAD = 4
N_LAYERS = 2
DIM_FF = 256
LR = 3e-4
LR_DECAY_FACTOR = 0.5

rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED)
ranks = np.arange(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, dtype=np.float32)
freq_weights = 1.0 / ranks
freq_weights /= freq_weights.sum()


def make_real_corpus(n=N_REAL, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    seqs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=seq_len, p=freq_weights) + 1
        seqs.append(tokens.tolist())
    return seqs


REAL_CORPUS = make_real_corpus()
sorted_by_freq = np.argsort(freq_weights)
n_tail = int(VOCAB_SIZE * TAIL_FRAC)
TAIL_TOKENS = set((sorted_by_freq[:n_tail] + 1).tolist())
print(
    f"Tail tokens: {len(TAIL_TOKENS)} tokens (indices {min(TAIL_TOKENS)}..{max(TAIL_TOKENS)})"
)


class SeqDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, seqs):
        self.data = [torch.tensor(s, dtype=torch.long) for s in seqs]

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return self.data[i]


class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, max_len=512):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        pos = torch.arange(0, max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        div = torch.exp(
            torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * (-math.log(10000.0) / d_model)
        )
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
        self.register_buffer("pe", pe.unsqueeze(0))

    def forward(self, x):
        return x + self.pe[:, : x.size(1)]


class SmallGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(VOCAB_SIZE + 1, D_MODEL, padding_idx=0)
        self.pe = PositionalEncoding(D_MODEL)
        enc_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            D_MODEL, N_HEAD, DIM_FF, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(enc_layer, num_layers=N_LAYERS)
        self.head = nn.Linear(D_MODEL, VOCAB_SIZE + 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        sz = x.size(1)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(sz, sz, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        e = self.pe(self.embed(x))
        out = self.transformer(e, mask=mask)
        return self.head(out)


def make_model():
    return SmallGPT().to(device)


def train_model(model, seqs, epochs=EPOCHS_PER_GEN, lr=LR):
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True, drop_last=False)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    model.train()
    losses = []
    for ep in range(epochs):
        ep_loss, n = 0.0, 0
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1), tgt.reshape(-1), ignore_index=0
            )
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * batch.size(0)
            n += batch.size(0)
        avg = ep_loss / n
        losses.append(avg)
        print(f"  epoch {ep+1}/{epochs} loss={avg:.4f}")
    return losses


@torch.no_grad()
def generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    model.eval()
    seqs = []
    bs = 128
    for start in range(0, n, bs):
        cur = min(bs, n - start)
        tokens = torch.randint(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, (cur, 1), device=device)
        for _ in range(seq_len - 1):
            logits = model(tokens)[:, -1, :]
            logits[:, 0] = -1e9
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            nxt = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            tokens = torch.cat([tokens, nxt], dim=1)
        seqs.extend(tokens.cpu().tolist())
    return seqs


def tail_token_coverage(seqs):
    seen = set()
    for s in seqs:
        seen.update(s)
    return len(seen & TAIL_TOKENS) / len(TAIL_TOKENS)


def compute_perplexity(model, seqs, max_seqs=500):
    model.eval()
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs[:max_seqs])
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)
    total_loss, total_tok = 0.0, 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1),
                tgt.reshape(-1),
                ignore_index=0,
                reduction="sum",
            )
            total_loss += loss.item()
            total_tok += (tgt != 0).sum().item()
    return math.exp(total_loss / max(total_tok, 1))


def token_freq_vector(seqs):
    counts = Counter(t for s in seqs for t in s)
    vec = np.zeros(VOCAB_SIZE + 1)
    for t, c in counts.items():
        vec[t] = c
    s = vec.sum()
    return vec / s if s > 0 else vec


def kl_divergence(p, q, eps=1e-10):
    mask = p > 0
    return float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log((p[mask] + eps) / (q[mask] + eps))))


REAL_FREQ = token_freq_vector(REAL_CORPUS)


def anchor_tail(real_corpus, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]


# Ablation: Model Reinitialization Strategy
reinit_strategies = {
    "Reinit (baseline)": "reinit",
    "Finetune (same LR)": "finetune_same",
    "Finetune (decay LR)": "finetune_decay",
}

experiment_data = {
    name: {
        "tail_coverage": [],
        "perplexity": [],
        "kl_divergence": [],
        "train_losses": [],
        "lrs_used": [],
    }
    for name in reinit_strategies
}

for strat_name, mode in reinit_strategies.items():
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"Reinitialization Strategy: {strat_name}")
    print("=" * 60)

    random.seed(SEED)
    np.random.seed(SEED)
    torch.manual_seed(SEED)

    model = make_model()
    current_lr = LR
    print(f"[Gen 0] Train on real corpus ({len(REAL_CORPUS)} seqs), LR={current_lr}")
    losses = train_model(model, REAL_CORPUS, lr=current_lr)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["train_losses"].append(losses)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["lrs_used"].append(current_lr)

    gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES)
    ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
    ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
    kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
    experiment_data[strat_name]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
    print(f"[Gen 0] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

    synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH)
    prev_model = model

    for gen in range(1, N_GENERATIONS + 1):
        print(f"\n[Gen {gen}]")
        if mode == "reinit":
            gen_lr = LR
        elif mode == "finetune_same":
            gen_lr = LR
        elif mode == "finetune_decay":
            gen_lr = LR * (LR_DECAY_FACTOR**gen)

        anchor = anchor_tail(REAL_CORPUS, ANCHOR_SIZE)
        train_seqs = anchor + synth_pool
        random.shuffle(train_seqs)
        print(f"  Training on {len(train_seqs)} sequences, LR={gen_lr:.6f}")

        if mode == "reinit":
            model = make_model()
        else:
            model = make_model()
            model.load_state_dict(prev_model.state_dict())

        losses = train_model(model, train_seqs, lr=gen_lr)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["train_losses"].append(losses)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["lrs_used"].append(gen_lr)

        gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES)
        ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
        ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
        kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
        experiment_data[strat_name]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
        print(f"[Gen {gen}] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

        synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH)
        prev_model = model

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print("\nExperiment data saved.")

# Plots
gens = list(range(N_GENERATIONS + 1))
colors = {
    "Reinit (baseline)": "blue",
    "Finetune (same LR)": "green",
    "Finetune (decay LR)": "orange",
}
linestyles = {
    "Reinit (baseline)": "-",
    "Finetune (same LR)": "--",
    "Finetune (decay LR)": "-.",
}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
fig.suptitle("Model Reinitialization Strategy Ablation (TADA anchor)", fontsize=13)
for strat_name in reinit_strategies:
    d = experiment_data[strat_name]
    c = colors[strat_name]
    ls = linestyles[strat_name]
    axes[0].plot(gens, d["tail_coverage"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat_name)
    axes[1].plot(gens, d["perplexity"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat_name)
    axes[2].plot(gens, d["kl_divergence"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat_name)
axes[0].set_title("Tail Token Coverage (↑)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Generation")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Coverage")
axes[1].set_title("Perplexity on Real Data (↓)")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Generation")
axes[1].set_ylabel("PPL")
axes[2].set_title("KL Divergence from Real (↓)")
axes[2].set_xlabel("Generation")
axes[2].set_ylabel("KL")
for ax in axes:
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
fname = os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_reinit_strategy.png")
plt.savefig(fname, dpi=150)
plt.close()
print(f"Plot saved: ablation_reinit_strategy.png")

# LR schedule plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
for strat_name in reinit_strategies:
    lrs = experiment_data[strat_name]["lrs_used"]
    ax.plot(
        gens,
        lrs,
        marker="o",
        label=strat_name,
        color=colors[strat_name],
        ls=linestyles[strat_name],
    )
ax.set_title("Learning Rate per Generation")
ax.set_xlabel("Generation")
ax.set_ylabel("Learning Rate")
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True)
ax.set_yscale("log")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_lr_schedule.png"), dpi=150)
plt.close()
print("Plot saved: ablation_lr_schedule.png")

# Training loss curves per generation per strategy
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(reinit_strategies), figsize=(18, 5))
fig.suptitle("Training Loss Curves per Generation", fontsize=13)
for ax, strat_name in zip(axes, reinit_strategies):
    d = experiment_data[strat_name]
    for g, loss_curve in enumerate(d["train_losses"]):

        )

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")

strategies = list(experiment_data.keys())
colors = {
    "Reinit (baseline)": "blue",
    "Finetune (same LR)": "green",
    "Finetune (decay LR)": "orange",
}
linestyles = {
    "Reinit (baseline)": "-",
    "Finetune (same LR)": "--",
    "Finetune (decay LR)": "-.",
}

# Plot 1: Main metrics across generations
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Model Reinitialization Strategy Ablation\n(Synthetic Text Dataset, TADA Anchor)",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    metrics = ["tail_coverage", "perplexity", "kl_divergence"]
    titles = [
        "Tail Token Coverage (↑)",
        "Perplexity on Real Data (↓)",
        "KL Divergence from Real (↓)",
    ]
    ylabels = ["Coverage", "PPL", "KL"]
    for ax, metric, title, ylabel in zip(axes, metrics, titles, ylabels):
        for strat in strategies:
            vals = experiment_data[strat][metric]
            gens = list(range(len(vals)))
            ax.plot(
                gens,
                vals,
                color=colors[strat],
                ls=linestyles[strat],
                marker="o",
                label=strat,
            )
        ax.set_title(title)
        ax.set_xlabel("Generation")
        ax.set_ylabel(ylabel)
        ax.legend(fontsize=8)
        ax.grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_ablation_main_metrics.png"), dpi=150
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: synthetic_text_ablation_main_metrics.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating main metrics plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 2: Learning rate schedule
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
    for strat in strategies:
        lrs = experiment_data[strat]["lrs_used"]
        gens = list(range(len(lrs)))
        plt.plot(
            gens,
            lrs,
            marker="o",
            label=strat,
            color=colors[strat],
            ls=linestyles[strat],
        )
    plt.title("Learning Rate per Generation\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)")
    plt.xlabel("Generation")
    plt.ylabel("Learning Rate")
    plt.yscale("log")
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_ablation_lr_schedule.png"), dpi=150
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: synthetic_text_ablation_lr_schedule.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating LR schedule plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 3: Training loss curves per strategy
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(strategies), figsize=(18, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Training Loss Curves per Generation\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)", fontsize=12
    )
    for ax, strat in zip(axes, strategies):
        train_losses = experiment_data[strat]["train_losses"]
        for g, loss_curve in enumerate(train_losses):
            ax.plot(
                range(1, len(loss_curve) + 1), loss_curve, marker="o", label=f"Gen {g}"
            )
        ax.set_title(strat, fontsize=10)
        ax.set_xlabel("Epoch")
        ax.set_ylabel("Loss")
        ax.legend(fontsize=7)
        ax.grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_ablation_train_losses.png"), dpi=150
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: synthetic_text_ablation_train_losses.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating training loss plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Final generation summary bar chart
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Final Generation (Gen 5) Summary\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)", fontsize=12
    )
    metric_pairs = [
        ("tail_coverage", "Tail Token Coverage (↑)"),
        ("perplexity", "Perplexity (↓)"),
        ("kl_divergence", "KL Divergence (↓)"),
    ]
    for ax, (metric, title) in zip(axes, metric_pairs):
        vals = [experiment_data[s][metric][-1] for s in strategies]
        bars = ax.bar(
            range(len(strategies)), vals, color=[colors[s] for s in strategies]
        )
        ax.set_xticks(range(len(strategies)))
        ax.set_xticklabels([s.replace(" ", "\n") for s in strategies], fontsize=8)
        ax.set_title(title)
        ax.grid(True, axis="y")
        for bar, val in zip(bars, vals):
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height(),
                f"{val:.3f}",
                ha="center",
                va="bottom",
                fontsize=8,
            )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_ablation_final_gen_summary.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: synthetic_text_ablation_final_gen_summary.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating summary bar chart: {e}")
    plt.close()
plot_plan: The experiment compares three model reinitialization strategies (Reinit baseline, Finetune same LR, Finetune decay LR) across 5 generations of synthetic data training, tracking tail token coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence. I'll load the experiment data and create visualizations for: (1) the three main metrics across generations for all strategies, (2) the learning rate schedule per strategy, and (3) training loss curves per generation per strategy. Each plot will be saved separately with descriptive names. The data structure contains per-strategy dictionaries with lists of metrics indexed by generation (0 through N_GENERATIONS). I'll plot all strategies on the same axes for comparison plots, and use subplots for per-strategy breakdowns. I'll handle potential data loading issues with try-except blocks and ensure all figures are properly closed after saving.

plot_analyses.analysis:
Across generations (0–5) with a TADA anchor, tail token coverage is essentially saturated (~1.0) for all strategies, with a single dip at Gen 2 (~0.98) that affects all curves equally. This suggests the tail-coverage metric as implemented is not sensitive enough in this regime (likely too coarse, too easy to satisfy, or dominated by anchor inclusion), so differences in collapse are better reflected by perplexity/KL.

Perplexity on real data increases with generation for all strategies (indicative of drift/collapse pressure), but the rate depends strongly on the reinitialization strategy:
- Finetune (decay LR) is consistently best (lowest PPL) and degrades slowest, ending around ~118 at Gen 5.
- Reinit baseline is intermediate, ending around ~121 at Gen 5.
- Finetune (same LR) is worst, with a sharp upward trend after Gen 2–3, ending around ~126 at Gen 5.

KL divergence from real also increases with generation, and the separation is even clearer:
- Finetune (decay LR) yields the lowest KL throughout later generations, ending ~0.058.
- Finetune (same LR) is next (~0.070).
- Reinit baseline is worst and accelerates after Gen 3–4, ending ~0.092.

Interpretation: under TADA anchoring, continuing training from the previous generation is beneficial only if the learning rate is annealed; otherwise, finetuning with the same LR appears to over-update on synthetic data and amplifies distributional drift (higher PPL and KL). Reinitialization avoids some of that compounding but still drifts more than LR-decayed finetuning. This is a useful ablation result: the ‘model carryover’ component is helpful, but it must be paired with a conservative optimization schedule to prevent synthetic-data overfitting.

Training loss curves per generation show distinct optimization dynamics:

- Reinit (baseline): each generation starts with a relatively high initial loss at epoch 1 and then drops steeply by epoch 2, followed by a plateau. Later generations start slightly lower than earlier ones, but the within-generation drop remains pronounced. This is consistent with repeatedly training from scratch: you pay an optimization “warm-up” cost every generation.

- Finetune (same LR): curves are much flatter within each generation—loss starts lower and decreases only slightly across epochs. This indicates the model is already near a local optimum for the training distribution at the start of each generation, but the lack of strong within-gen improvement combined with the earlier plot’s worsening PPL/KL suggests it may be converging to a synthetic-biased optimum (i.e., stable training loss does not imply alignment with real distribution).

- Finetune (decay LR): similarly flat curves, but with slightly better (lower) loss levels than same-LR finetuning in later generations. This matches the better PPL/KL: smaller step sizes likely reduce catastrophic drift while still allowing modest adaptation.

Overall, these curves support the hypothesis that the main issue is not optimization failure but distributional shift: finetuning can look ‘well-behaved’ in training loss while still collapsing w.r.t. real-data metrics. The decay-LR schedule seems to provide a regularization-like effect across generations.

This set repeats the same three metrics for the synthetic text dataset with TADA anchor and shows the same qualitative story: tail token coverage is saturated (~1.0) with a shared dip at Gen 2 (~0.98), while PPL and KL separate the strategies.

Perplexity: decay-LR finetuning remains best and ends lowest; reinit is in the middle; same-LR finetuning is worst and increases fastest.

KL: decay-LR finetuning is best (lowest), same-LR is intermediate, and reinit is worst, with reinit showing a notable jump after Gen 3–4.

This consistency suggests the effect is robust to the dataset setting shown here (synthetic text dataset) and not an artifact of a single plot. It strengthens the ablation claim that ‘carry over weights + LR decay’ is the most stable reinitialization strategy under iterative synthetic training even when using tail-aware anchoring.

Final Gen 5 summary bars quantify the end-state differences:
- Tail token coverage: all methods at 1.000, confirming the metric saturates and cannot distinguish strategies at Gen 5.
- Perplexity: Finetune (decay LR) is best (117.907), Reinit is next (120.758), Finetune (same LR) is worst (125.944). The gap between decay-LR and same-LR is substantial (~8 PPL), and decay-LR improves over reinit by ~2.85 PPL.
- KL divergence: Finetune (decay LR) is best (0.058), Finetune (same LR) is next (0.070), Reinit is worst (0.092). The relative reduction in KL from reinit to decay-LR is large (~37% reduction), indicating materially better distributional fidelity.

Takeaway: the dominant gains from the ablation show up in KL (distributional match) more than PPL, which is aligned with the collapse narrative (mode/tail erosion manifests as distributional divergence). This bar chart is a strong, paper-friendly summary for the reinitialization-strategy ablation.

Learning-rate schedule per generation clarifies the experimental manipulation:
- Reinit and Finetune (same LR) keep LR constant across generations (flat line).
- Finetune (decay LR) reduces LR roughly exponentially each generation, reaching ~1e-5 by Gen 5.

This provides a plausible mechanistic explanation for the earlier results: as synthetic generations progress, the synthetic dataset becomes increasingly self-referential; decaying LR reduces the effective ‘trust’ in later-generation synthetic gradients, preventing runaway drift. In contrast, constant LR keeps updates aggressive even when the data quality/diversity may be degrading, which matches the observed worsening PPL/KL for same-LR finetuning.

For the ablation narrative, this plot supports the claim that optimization schedule is a critical component when leveraging weight carryover in iterative synthetic training.

Training loss curves on the synthetic text dataset mirror the earlier loss-curve panel:
- Reinit: steep within-generation loss drops (epoch 1→2) and then plateau, consistent with repeated from-scratch optimization.
- Finetune (same LR): relatively flat curves with small improvements across epochs; despite stable training loss, this strategy performs poorly on real-data PPL/KL, reinforcing that training loss is not a reliable collapse indicator.
- Finetune (decay LR): flat curves but generally slightly better loss levels than same-LR finetuning in later generations, consistent with better real-data metrics.

The key insight remains: stability/low loss on the synthetic training set does not guarantee preservation of the real distribution; decay-LR finetuning best aligns optimization dynamics with real-data fidelity.

This learning-rate plot appears to be a duplicate of the earlier LR-per-generation figure, again showing constant LR for reinit and same-LR finetuning and exponential decay for the decay-LR finetuning.

The duplication itself is fine for a report but for a paper you likely only need one instance; more importantly, it reinforces that the main differentiator in the best-performing strategy is the LR annealing across generations, not just the choice to finetune vs reinitialize.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots show a reinitialization-strategy ablation under TADA anchoring across generations. Tail token coverage saturates (~1.0) and is not discriminative (shared dip at Gen 2). Real-data perplexity and KL divergence increase with generation, but finetuning with LR decay consistently yields the lowest PPL/KL (best stability), reinit is intermediate, and finetuning with the same LR is worst (largest drift). Training loss curves are stable for finetuning but do not correlate with real-data fidelity, highlighting distributional shift; LR decay likely prevents over-updating on later-generation synthetic data. Final Gen 5 bars quantify sizable improvements in KL (≈37% vs reinit) and PPL (≈8 vs same-LR finetune).
ablation_name: Model Reinitialization vs. Fine-tuning Between Generations

overall_plan: This research investigates model collapse and tail token coverage across multiple generations of synthetic data training, using three strategies: TADA tail anchor, No anchor collapse, and Golden-ratio baseline. The work proceeds in two phases. First, a hyperparameter tuning phase identifies the optimal learning rate by testing four values (1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3) across all strategies, tracking tail coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence to select the best LR for subsequent experiments. Building on this foundation, the primary scientific contribution is an ablation study on temperature scaling during synthetic data generation. This ablation varies the generation temperature (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0) across all three strategies over 5 generations of training, examining how sampling temperature affects model collapse dynamics and tail token coverage. For each temperature-strategy combination, a fresh model is trained and synthetic data is generated at the specified temperature, with tail coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence tracked across generations. Results are saved as numpy arrays and visualized through per-temperature plots, per-strategy temperature comparison plots, aggregate summary bars, and heatmaps, enabling a comprehensive understanding of how generation temperature interacts with different training strategies to either mitigate or accelerate model collapse.

code:
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
import numpy as np
import random
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import Counter

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)

VOCAB_SIZE = 500
SEQ_LEN = 32
N_REAL = 2000
ANCHOR_SIZE = 200
BATCH_SIZE = 64
EPOCHS_PER_GEN = 5
N_GENERATIONS = 5
N_SYNTH = 2000
GEN_SAMPLES = 500
TAIL_FRAC = 0.10
D_MODEL = 128
N_HEAD = 4
N_LAYERS = 2
DIM_FF = 256
LR = 3e-4
TEMPERATURES = [0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0]

rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED)
ranks = np.arange(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, dtype=np.float32)
freq_weights = 1.0 / ranks
freq_weights /= freq_weights.sum()


def make_real_corpus(n=N_REAL, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    seqs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=seq_len, p=freq_weights) + 1
        seqs.append(tokens.tolist())
    return seqs


REAL_CORPUS = make_real_corpus()

sorted_by_freq = np.argsort(freq_weights)
n_tail = int(VOCAB_SIZE * TAIL_FRAC)
TAIL_TOKENS = set((sorted_by_freq[:n_tail] + 1).tolist())
print(
    f"Tail tokens: {len(TAIL_TOKENS)} tokens (indices {min(TAIL_TOKENS)}..{max(TAIL_TOKENS)})"
)


class SeqDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, seqs):
        self.data = [torch.tensor(s, dtype=torch.long) for s in seqs]

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return self.data[i]


class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, max_len=512):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        pos = torch.arange(0, max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        div = torch.exp(
            torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * (-math.log(10000.0) / d_model)
        )
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
        self.register_buffer("pe", pe.unsqueeze(0))

    def forward(self, x):
        return x + self.pe[:, : x.size(1)]


class SmallGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(VOCAB_SIZE + 1, D_MODEL, padding_idx=0)
        self.pe = PositionalEncoding(D_MODEL)
        enc_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            D_MODEL, N_HEAD, DIM_FF, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(enc_layer, num_layers=N_LAYERS)
        self.head = nn.Linear(D_MODEL, VOCAB_SIZE + 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        sz = x.size(1)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(sz, sz, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        e = self.pe(self.embed(x))
        out = self.transformer(e, mask=mask)
        return self.head(out)


def make_model():
    return SmallGPT().to(device)


def train_model(model, seqs, epochs=EPOCHS_PER_GEN, lr=LR):
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True, drop_last=False)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    model.train()
    losses = []
    for ep in range(epochs):
        ep_loss = 0.0
        n = 0
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1), tgt.reshape(-1), ignore_index=0
            )
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * batch.size(0)
            n += batch.size(0)
        avg = ep_loss / n
        losses.append(avg)
        print(f"  epoch {ep+1}/{epochs} loss={avg:.4f}")
    return losses


@torch.no_grad()
def generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, seq_len=SEQ_LEN, temperature=1.0):
    model.eval()
    seqs = []
    bs = 128
    for start in range(0, n, bs):
        cur = min(bs, n - start)
        tokens = torch.randint(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, (cur, 1), device=device)
        for _ in range(seq_len - 1):
            logits = model(tokens)[:, -1, :]
            logits[:, 0] = -1e9
            scaled_logits = logits / temperature
            probs = F.softmax(scaled_logits, dim=-1)
            nxt = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            tokens = torch.cat([tokens, nxt], dim=1)
        seqs.extend(tokens.cpu().tolist())
    return seqs


def tail_token_coverage(seqs):
    seen = set()
    for s in seqs:
        seen.update(s)
    return len(seen & TAIL_TOKENS) / len(TAIL_TOKENS)


def compute_perplexity(model, seqs, max_seqs=500):
    model.eval()
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs[:max_seqs])
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)
    total_loss = 0.0
    total_tok = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1),
                tgt.reshape(-1),
                ignore_index=0,
                reduction="sum",
            )
            total_loss += loss.item()
            total_tok += (tgt != 0).sum().item()
    return math.exp(total_loss / max(total_tok, 1))


def token_freq_vector(seqs):
    counts = Counter(t for s in seqs for t in s)
    vec = np.zeros(VOCAB_SIZE + 1)
    for t, c in counts.items():
        vec[t] = c
    s = vec.sum()
    return vec / s if s > 0 else vec


def kl_divergence(p, q, eps=1e-10):
    mask = p > 0
    return float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log((p[mask] + eps) / (q[mask] + eps))))


REAL_FREQ = token_freq_vector(REAL_CORPUS)


def anchor_tail(real_corpus, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]


GOLDEN_REAL_FRAC = 0.38

strategies_subset = {
    "TADA (tail anchor)": anchor_tail,
    "No anchor (collapse)": None,
    "Golden-ratio baseline": None,
}

experiment_data = {}

for temp in TEMPERATURES:
    temp_key = f"temp_{temp}"
    print(f"\n{'#'*70}")
    print(f"# Generation Temperature: {temp}")
    print(f"{'#'*70}")

    experiment_data[temp_key] = {
        name: {
            "tail_coverage": [],
            "perplexity": [],
            "kl_divergence": [],
            "train_losses": [],
        }
        for name in strategies_subset
    }

    for strat_name, anchor_fn in strategies_subset.items():
        print(f"\n{'='*60}")
        print(f"Strategy: {strat_name} | Temperature: {temp}")
        print("=" * 60)

        random.seed(SEED)
        np.random.seed(SEED)
        torch.manual_seed(SEED)

        model = make_model()
        print(f"[Gen 0] Train on real corpus ({len(REAL_CORPUS)} seqs)")
        losses = train_model(model, REAL_CORPUS, lr=LR)
        experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["train_losses"].append(losses)

        gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES, temperature=temp)
        ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
        ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
        kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
        experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
        experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
        experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
        print(f"[Gen 0] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

        synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, temperature=temp)

        for gen in range(1, N_GENERATIONS + 1):
            print(f"\n[Gen {gen}]")
            if strat_name == "No anchor (collapse)":
                train_seqs = synth_pool
            elif strat_name == "Golden-ratio baseline":
                n_real_mix = int(GOLDEN_REAL_FRAC * (N_SYNTH + ANCHOR_SIZE))
                n_real_mix = min(n_real_mix, len(REAL_CORPUS))
                real_mix = random.sample(REAL_CORPUS, n_real_mix)
                train_seqs = real_mix + synth_pool
            else:
                anchor = anchor_fn(REAL_CORPUS, ANCHOR_SIZE)
                train_seqs = anchor + synth_pool

            random.shuffle(train_seqs)
            print(f"  Training on {len(train_seqs)} sequences")
            model = make_model()
            losses = train_model(model, train_seqs, lr=LR)
            experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["train_losses"].append(losses)

            gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES, temperature=temp)
            ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
            ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
            kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
            experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
            experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
            experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
            print(f"[Gen {gen}] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

            synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, temperature=temp)

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print("\nExperiment data saved.")

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")

TEMPERATURES = [0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0]
strategies_subset = [
    "TADA (tail anchor)",
    "No anchor (collapse)",
    "Golden-ratio baseline",
]
colors = {
    "TADA (tail anchor)": "green",
    "No anchor (collapse)": "red",
    "Golden-ratio baseline": "purple",
}
linestyles = {
    "TADA (tail anchor)": "-",
    "No anchor (collapse)": "-",
    "Golden-ratio baseline": ":",
}
temp_line_styles = ["-", "--", "-.", ":"]
temp_markers = ["o", "s", "^", "D"]
temp_colors = ["blue", "green", "orange", "red"]
N_GENERATIONS = 5
gens = list(range(N_GENERATIONS + 1))

# Plot 1: Per-temperature metrics (all strategies)
for temp in TEMPERATURES:
    try:
        temp_key = f"temp_{temp}"
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
        fig.suptitle(
            f"Iterative Synthetic Training Metrics | Generation Temperature = {temp}",
            fontsize=13,
        )
        for strat_name in strategies_subset:
            d = experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]
            c = colors[strat_name]
            ls = linestyles[strat_name]
            axes[0].plot(
                gens, d["tail_coverage"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat_name
            )
            axes[1].plot(
                gens, d["perplexity"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat_name
            )
            axes[2].plot(
                gens, d["kl_divergence"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat_name
            )
        axes[0].set_title("Tail Token Coverage (↑ better)")
        axes[0].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[0].set_ylabel("Coverage")
        axes[1].set_title("Perplexity on Real Data (↓ better)")
        axes[1].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[1].set_ylabel("PPL")
        axes[2].set_title("KL Divergence from Real (↓ better)")
        axes[2].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[2].set_ylabel("KL")
        for ax in axes:
            ax.legend(fontsize=7)
            ax.grid(True)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, f"metrics_all_strategies_temp_{temp}.png"),
            dpi=150,
        )
        plt.close()
        print(f"Saved metrics_all_strategies_temp_{temp}.png")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error in per-temp plot temp={temp}: {e}")
        plt.close()

# Plot 2: Per-strategy temperature comparison
for strat_name in strategies_subset:
    try:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
        safe_name = (
            strat_name.replace(" ", "_")
            .replace("(", "")
            .replace(")", "")
            .replace("/", "")
        )
        fig.suptitle(f"Temperature Comparison | Strategy: {strat_name}", fontsize=12)
        for i, temp in enumerate(TEMPERATURES):
            temp_key = f"temp_{temp}"
            d = experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]
            axes[0].plot(
                gens,
                d["tail_coverage"],
                ls=temp_line_styles[i],
                marker=temp_markers[i],
                color=temp_colors[i],
                label=f"Temp={temp}",
            )
            axes[1].plot(
                gens,
                d["perplexity"],
                ls=temp_line_styles[i],
                marker=temp_markers[i],
                color=temp_colors[i],
                label=f"Temp={temp}",
            )
            axes[2].plot(
                gens,
                d["kl_divergence"],
                ls=temp_line_styles[i],
                marker=temp_markers[i],
                color=temp_colors[i],
                label=f"Temp={temp}",
            )
        axes[0].set_title("Tail Token Coverage (↑ better)")
        axes[0].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[0].set_ylabel("Coverage")
        axes[1].set_title("Perplexity on Real Data (↓ better)")
        axes[1].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[1].set_ylabel("PPL")
        axes[2].set_title("KL Divergence from Real (↓ better)")
        axes[2].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[2].set_ylabel("KL")
        for ax in axes:
            ax.legend(fontsize=8)
            ax.grid(True)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, f"temp_comparison_{safe_name}.png"), dpi=150
        )
        plt.close()
        print(f"Saved temp_comparison_{safe_name}.png")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error in per-strategy plot {strat_name}: {e}")
        plt.close()

# Plot 3: Aggregate summary bar chart (final generation, averaged across strategies)
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Temperature Ablation: Final Generation Metrics Averaged Across All Strategies",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    metrics = ["tail_coverage", "kl_divergence", "perplexity"]
    metric_labels = [
        "Tail Coverage (↑ better)",
        "KL Divergence (↓ better)",
        "Perplexity (↓ better)",
    ]
    for mi, (metric, mlabel) in enumerate(zip(metrics, metric_labels)):
        avg_vals = []
        for temp in TEMPERATURES:
            temp_key = f"temp_{temp}"
            vals = [experiment_data[temp_key][s][metric][-1] for s in strategies_subset]
            avg_vals.append(np.mean(vals))
        axes[mi].bar([str(t) for t in TEMPERATURES], avg_vals, color=temp_colors)
        axes[mi].set_title(mlabel)
        axes[mi].set_xlabel("Temperature")
        axes[mi].set_ylabel(metric.replace("_", " ").title())
        axes[mi].grid(True, axis="y")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "temperature_ablation_summary_bar.png"), dpi=150
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved temperature_ablation_summary_bar.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error in summary bar chart: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Heatmaps for TADA strategy (Temperature vs Generation)
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Heatmap: Temperature vs Generation | Strategy: TADA (tail anchor)", fontsize=12
    )
    strat_name = "TADA (tail anchor)"
    metric_names = ["tail_coverage", "kl_divergence", "perplexity"]
    metric_labels_heat = [
        "Tail Coverage (↑ better)",
        "KL Divergence (↓ better)",
        "Perplexity (↓ better)",
    ]
    for mi, (metric, mlabel) in enumerate(zip(metric_names, metric_labels_heat)):
        matrix = np.zeros((len(TEMPERATURES), len(gens)))
        for ti, temp in enumerate(TEMPERATURES):
            temp_key = f"temp_{temp}"
            matrix[ti, :] = experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name][metric]
        im = axes[mi].imshow(matrix, aspect="auto", cmap="viridis")
        axes[mi].set_xticks(range(len(gens)))
        axes[mi].set_xticklabels([str(g) for g in gens])
        axes[mi].set_yticks(range(len(TEMPERATURES)))
        axes[mi].set_yticklabels([str(t) for t in TEMPERATURES])
        axes[mi].set_xlabel("Generation")
        axes[mi].set_ylabel("Temperature")
        axes[mi].set_title(mlabel)
        plt.colorbar(im, ax=axes[mi])
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "heatmap_TADA_temp_vs_generation.png"), dpi=150
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved heatmap_TADA_temp_vs_generation.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error in TADA heatmap: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 5: Tail coverage degradation (G0→GN) vs temperature per strategy
try:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
    for strat_name in strategies_subset:
        deltas = []
        for temp in TEMPERATURES:
            temp_key = f"temp_{temp}"
            d = experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]
            delta = d["tail_coverage"][-1] - d["tail_coverage"][0]
            deltas.append(delta)
        ax.plot(
            TEMPERATURES,
            deltas,
            marker="o",
            color=colors[strat_name],
            ls=linestyles[strat_name],
            label=strat_name,
        )
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", ls="--", alpha=0.5)
    ax.set_title(
        "Tail Coverage Change (Generation 0 → Final) vs Temperature\nNegative = Degradation (Model Collapse Risk)"
    )
    ax.set_xlabel("Temperature")
    ax.set_ylabel("ΔTail Coverage (GN - G0)")
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "tail_coverage_degradation_vs_temperature.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved tail_coverage_degradation_vs_temperature.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error in tail coverage degradation plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 6: Training loss curves for each strategy at temp=1.0 (select up to 5 generations at interval)
try:
    temp_key = "temp_1.0"
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(strategies_subset), figsize=(16, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Training Loss Curves per Generation | Temperature=1.0 | All Strategies",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    total_gens = N_GENERATIONS + 1  # gens 0..5
    plot_gens = list(range(0, total_gens, max(1, total_gens // 5)))[:5]
    for si, strat_name in enumerate(strategies_subset):
        d = experiment_data[temp_key][strat_name]
        train_losses = d["train_losses"]
        for gi in plot_gens:
            if gi < len(train_losses):
                axes[si].plot(
                    range(1, len(train_losses[gi]) + 1),
                    train_losses[gi],
                    marker="o",
                    label=f"Gen {gi}",
                )
        axes[si].set_title(f"{strat_name}", fontsize=9)
        axes[si].set_xlabel("Epoch")
        axes[si].set_ylabel("Train Loss")
        axes[si].legend(fontsize=7)
        axes[si].grid(True)

    )

    plt.close()
plot_plan: The experiment explores model collapse in iterative synthetic data training, comparing three strategies (TADA tail anchor, No anchor collapse, Golden-ratio baseline) across four generation temperatures (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0) over 5 generations. I'll load the experiment data and create several visualizations: per-temperature metric plots showing all strategies, per-strategy temperature comparison plots, a summary bar chart of final-generation metrics averaged across strategies, heatmaps for the TADA strategy, and a tail coverage degradation plot. Each plot will be in its own try-except block for safety. The key metrics tracked are tail token coverage, perplexity on real data, and KL divergence from the real token distribution. I'll also plot training loss curves for a representative subset to avoid too many figures.

plot_analyses.analysis: Final-generation metrics vs temperature (averaged across strategies): Temperature=0.5 is clearly pathological: tail coverage is much lower (~0.62) while KL divergence is extremely high (~7) and perplexity is extremely high (~2400), indicating severe distributional drift/collapse at low sampling temperature. Temperatures 1.0–2.0 all yield saturated tail coverage (~1.0), but differ on fidelity: temperature=1.0 achieves the lowest KL (~0.05) and lowest perplexity (~120), while 1.5 and 2.0 worsen both KL (~1.0–1.3) and perplexity (~220–270). Net: there is a sharp phase transition between 0.5 and ≥1.0, and an optimal region around temperature≈1.0 for maintaining alignment to real data while preserving tails.

Duplicate of the prior bar-chart summary (same three panels). The repetition suggests the figure was exported twice with slightly different titles; the substantive takeaway remains: avoid low temperature (0.5) due to collapse; temperature 1.0 is best on KL/PPL while keeping tail coverage maxed; higher temperatures preserve tail coverage but degrade KL/PPL.

Tail coverage change (G0→final) vs temperature: At temperature=0.5, both TADA and no-anchor show negative Δtail coverage (≈−0.04 for TADA, ≈−0.12 for no-anchor), indicating measurable tail erosion even with tail anchoring, and much worse without anchors. From temperature=1.0 onward, Δtail coverage is ~0 for all shown strategies (TADA, no-anchor, golden-ratio), implying that in this setup tail coverage is stable when sampling is not overly conservative. This suggests that sampling temperature can dominate collapse dynamics; anchors matter most in the low-temperature/low-diversity regime.

Duplicate of the prior Δtail coverage vs temperature plot (same curves/values). Confirms robustness of the qualitative pattern: only temperature=0.5 shows tail degradation, and TADA mitigates but does not fully eliminate it; higher temperatures eliminate degradation in this metric.

Heatmaps over generation×temperature for TADA: Tail coverage is near-saturated (≈1.0) across generations for temperatures 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, with only temperature=0.5 showing consistently lower coverage (~0.62–0.70) and some fluctuation. KL divergence under TADA is minimal at temperature=1.0 across generations (dark/low), while temperature=0.5 yields very high KL that increases with generation (strong drift), and temperatures 1.5–2.0 show moderate KL that tends to increase with generation. Perplexity mirrors KL: best at temperature=1.0 (lowest and stable), catastrophic spike at temperature=0.5 (notably at generation 1) and then remaining high, and intermediate degradation for 1.5–2.0. Overall, for TADA the temperature knob trades off diversity vs fidelity, but too-low temperature causes compounding drift/collapse despite anchoring.

Duplicate of the TADA heatmaps (same three panels). Reinforces that temperature=1.0 is the stable sweet spot for both distributional alignment (KL) and downstream quality (PPL), while 0.5 is unstable/collapsing and 1.5–2.0 introduce increasing divergence over generations.

Training loss curves per generation at temperature=1.0 (all strategies): All strategies show typical within-generation training behavior—steep loss drop from epoch 1→2 then plateau. Across generations, losses decrease (later generations start lower and end lower), suggesting the training objective is being optimized more easily over time, which can be consistent with synthetic self-training making the data distribution simpler. Importantly, the no-anchor strategy achieves the lowest final training loss by later generations (≈4.34 at Gen4) compared to TADA (≈4.47) and golden-ratio (≈4.59). This is a red flag: lower training loss does not imply better real-data generalization; it may indicate overfitting to increasingly self-generated, low-entropy synthetic data (a hallmark of collapse). This plot therefore supports the need for external metrics (PPL on real, KL to real, tail coverage) rather than relying on train loss.

Iterative synthetic training metrics at temperature=1.0: Tail token coverage stays essentially at 1.0 for all three strategies across generations, with small dips for TADA at Gen2 (~0.98) and for no-anchor at Gen4 (~0.98), suggesting this tail metric may be too coarse/saturated at this temperature. Perplexity on real data increases with generation for TADA and no-anchor (TADA ~114.6→~120.7; no-anchor ~114.6→~122.2), but remains much flatter and lower for the golden-ratio baseline (~114.6→~116.5). KL divergence from real rises for all, but golden-ratio stays lowest (~0.018→~0.035), no-anchor rises more (~0.018→~0.083), and TADA is intermediate early but ends slightly worse than no-anchor at Gen5 (~0.092 vs ~0.083). This suggests that at temperature=1.0, simple mixing-ratio anchoring (golden-ratio) is outperforming the current TADA implementation on global distributional alignment, and that the chosen TADA anchor size/weighting may be insufficient or the tail-selection proxy may not be capturing the right notion of tail for preventing KL drift.

Temperature comparison over generations for no-anchor: Temperature=0.5 collapses strongly: tail coverage declines from ~0.68 to ~0.54–0.56 by later generations, perplexity explodes to ~7000 by Gen5, and KL grows to ~8–8.5—classic collapse/degeneration. Temperature=1.0 is stable and best: tail coverage ~1.0, perplexity ~110–120, KL ~0.05–0.1. Temperatures 1.5 and 2.0 maintain tail coverage ~1.0 but show steadily increasing perplexity (~200–500) and KL (~0.3–1.4), indicating drift away from real distribution despite not losing tail tokens. This cleanly demonstrates that sampling temperature is a primary driver of collapse risk and that tail coverage alone can miss substantial degradation captured by KL/PPL.

Temperature comparison over generations for TADA: Temperature=0.5 again underperforms: tail coverage ~0.62–0.68, perplexity spikes sharply at Gen1 (~215) then settles ~140–150, while KL rises steeply to ~6–8 by Gen3 and remains high—showing that tail anchoring does not rescue extremely low-temperature sampling. Temperature=1.0 is consistently best: tail coverage ~1.0 (minor dip ~0.98 at Gen2), perplexity stays low (~115→~121), and KL remains near zero with slight growth. Temperatures 1.5 and 2.0 keep tail coverage at 1.0 but have higher perplexity (~130–175) and KL (~0.2–1.3) that increase with generation. Compared to no-anchor, TADA at temp=0.5 substantially reduces perplexity blow-up (no-anchor reaches thousands; TADA stays ~150) but KL is still very large, implying TADA may prevent catastrophic loss of fluency while still drifting distributionally. Net: TADA provides robustness benefits in the hardest regime (low temp), but temperature=1.0 remains the recommended operating point for stable iterative training.

vlm_feedback_summary: Received multiple plots from a temperature ablation and iterative synthetic training study comparing TADA (tail anchor), no-anchor (collapse), and golden-ratio baseline. Plots include: final-generation bar charts vs temperature for tail coverage/KL/perplexity; tail coverage change vs temperature; heatmaps over generation×temperature for TADA; training loss curves per generation at temperature=1.0; iterative metrics over generations at temperature=1.0; and per-strategy temperature comparisons over generations.
ablation_name: Temperature Scaling During Synthetic Data Generation

overall_plan: This research investigates improvements to the TADA framework through two complementary experiments. First, a hyperparameter tuning phase identifies the optimal learning rate (testing 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3) across all strategies, tracking tail coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence to determine the best LR for final generation metrics. Building on this foundation, the main scientific investigation is an ablation study on 'Anchor Diversity Enforcement via Token Coverage Filtering,' which examines whether enforcing diversity within the anchor set improves model training. The ablation compares four anchor selection strategies: (1) the original TADA tail-frequency selection, (2) greedy full-vocabulary diversity selection, (3) greedy tail-token diversity selection, and (4) a random baseline. Each strategy selects ANCHOR_SIZE sequences from the real corpus using different criteria, and the experiment tracks tail token coverage, perplexity, KL divergence, and anchor set characteristics across N_GENERATIONS of iterative training. The core scientific question is whether diversity-enforced anchor selection leads to better tail token coverage and overall model quality compared to the original tail-frequency approach in TADA.
