import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")

strategies = list(experiment_data.keys())
colors = {
    "Shuffled (baseline)": "blue",
    "Anchor-first curriculum": "green",
    "Synth-first curriculum": "orange",
}
linestyles = {
    "Shuffled (baseline)": "--",
    "Anchor-first curriculum": "-",
    "Synth-first curriculum": "-.",
}

# Plot 1: All three metrics across generations
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Curriculum Strategy Comparison Across Generations\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    for strat in strategies:
        d = experiment_data[strat]
        gens = list(range(len(d["tail_coverage"])))
        c, ls = colors[strat], linestyles[strat]
        axes[0].plot(gens, d["tail_coverage"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat)
        axes[1].plot(gens, d["perplexity"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat)
        axes[2].plot(gens, d["kl_divergence"], color=c, ls=ls, marker="o", label=strat)
    for ax, title, ylabel in zip(
        axes,
        [
            "Tail Token Coverage (↑)",
            "Perplexity on Real Data (↓)",
            "KL Divergence from Real (↓)",
        ],
        ["Coverage", "PPL", "KL"],
    ):
        ax.set_title(title)
        ax.set_xlabel("Generation")
        ax.set_ylabel(ylabel)
        ax.legend(fontsize=8)
        ax.grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_metrics_across_generations.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating metrics plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 2: Training loss curves per strategy (all generations overlaid)
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(strategies), figsize=(18, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Training Loss Per Generation by Curriculum Strategy\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    for idx, strat in enumerate(strategies):
        ax = axes[idx]
        all_losses = experiment_data[strat]["train_losses"]
        for gen_idx, gen_losses in enumerate(all_losses):
            ax.plot(
                range(1, len(gen_losses) + 1),
                gen_losses,
                marker="o",
                label=f"Gen {gen_idx}",
            )
        ax.set_title(strat, fontsize=9)
        ax.set_xlabel("Epoch")
        ax.set_ylabel("Loss")
        ax.legend(fontsize=7)
        ax.grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_training_loss_per_strategy.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating loss plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 3: Bar chart of final generation metrics
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Final Generation Metric Comparison Across Curriculum Strategies\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    short_names = ["Shuffled", "Anchor-first", "Synth-first"]
    bar_colors = [colors[s] for s in strategies]
    for ax, metric, title, ylabel in zip(
        axes,
        ["tail_coverage", "perplexity", "kl_divergence"],
        ["Tail Token Coverage (↑)", "Perplexity (↓)", "KL Divergence (↓)"],
        ["Coverage", "PPL", "KL"],
    ):
        vals = [experiment_data[s][metric][-1] for s in strategies]
        bars = ax.bar(short_names, vals, color=bar_colors)
        ax.set_title(title)
        ax.set_ylabel(ylabel)
        ax.grid(True, axis="y")
        for bar, val in zip(bars, vals):
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height(),
                f"{val:.3f}",
                ha="center",
                va="bottom",
                fontsize=9,
            )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_final_generation_bar_comparison.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating bar plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Heatmap of all metrics across all generations
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Metric Heatmap: All Strategies × Generations\n(Synthetic Text Dataset)",
        fontsize=12,
    )
    for ax, metric, title in zip(
        axes,
        ["tail_coverage", "perplexity", "kl_divergence"],
        ["Tail Token Coverage", "Perplexity", "KL Divergence"],
    ):
        matrix = np.array([experiment_data[s][metric] for s in strategies])
        im = ax.imshow(matrix, aspect="auto", cmap="viridis")
        ax.set_title(title)
        ax.set_xlabel("Generation")
        ax.set_ylabel("Strategy")
        ax.set_yticks(range(len(strategies)))
        ax.set_yticklabels(["Shuffled", "Anchor-first", "Synth-first"], fontsize=8)
        ax.set_xticks(range(matrix.shape[1]))
        plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax)
        for i in range(matrix.shape[0]):
            for j in range(matrix.shape[1]):
                ax.text(
                    j,
                    i,
                    f"{matrix[i,j]:.2f}",
                    ha="center",
                    va="center",
                    fontsize=7,
                    color="white",
                )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "synthetic_text_metrics_heatmap.png"), dpi=150
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating heatmap: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Print summary metrics
print("\nFinal Generation Summary:")
print(f"{'Strategy':<30} {'TailCov':>10} {'PPL':>8} {'KL':>8}")
for s in strategies:
    d = experiment_data[s]
    print(
        f"{s:<30} {d['tail_coverage'][-1]:>10.4f} {d['perplexity'][-1]:>8.2f} {d['kl_divergence'][-1]:>8.4f}"
    )
plot_plan: The experiment compares three curriculum strategies (Shuffled baseline, Anchor-first, Synth-first) for training a small GPT model across multiple generations of synthetic data. The key metrics tracked are tail token coverage, perplexity on real data, and KL divergence from real token distribution. I'll load the experiment data and create several visualizations: (1) line plots showing all three metrics across generations for all strategies, (2) training loss curves per generation for each strategy, (3) a bar chart comparing final generation performance, and (4) a heatmap summarizing metric values across all generations and strategies. Each plot will be saved separately with descriptive names. I'll use try-except blocks around each plot and only plot training loss at suitable intervals to avoid overcrowding.

plot_analyses.analysis:
Tail token coverage is essentially saturated for all curriculum orders across generations (≈1.00 everywhere), with a single dip at generation 2 to ≈0.98 that is shared by all strategies. This indicates the current tail-coverage metric (as implemented) is not sensitive enough to distinguish collapse dynamics under this ablation; either the tail set is too easy to cover, the thresholding/definition is too coarse, or the evaluation sample size is large enough that coverage saturates. As a result, conclusions about “tail preservation” cannot be supported from these plots alone; the signal is dominated by ceiling effects.

Perplexity on real data increases monotonically with generation for all strategies, suggesting progressive distribution drift/collapse under iterative synthetic training. Differences emerge late: by generation 5, Synth-first has the lowest PPL (~118.9), Shuffled is intermediate (~120.8), and Anchor-first is worst (~121.8). The gap is modest but consistent in the heatmap and line plots, implying curriculum order matters for stability/generalization, but in the opposite direction one might expect if “anchoring early” were beneficial.

KL divergence from real also increases with generation for all strategies, with a pronounced jump after generation 3→4. Synth-first again is best (lowest KL), Shuffled next, Anchor-first worst. At generation 5: Synth-first ~0.075 vs Shuffled ~0.092 vs Anchor-first ~0.095. This aligns with the PPL ordering and supports that Synth-first maintains closer distributional match to real data across generations.

Overall, this ablation suggests that, given a fixed anchor set and mixing ratio, presenting synthetic data earlier (Synth-first) yields better long-horizon alignment than Anchor-first or Shuffled. A plausible mechanism is that early exposure to synthetic distribution reduces later overfitting to a small anchor subset, while still benefiting from anchors later as a corrective signal. Alternatively, Anchor-first may cause the model to “lock in” on anchor idiosyncrasies, making subsequent synthetic training amplify drift.

Training loss curves per epoch show normal within-generation convergence and a consistent downward shift in loss for later generations (Gen 0 highest, Gen 5 lowest) across all curricula. This indicates the model is fitting its training data increasingly well over generations even as real-data PPL/KL worsen—classic signature of collapse/over-specialization to synthetic data. Among curricula, Anchor-first appears to achieve slightly lower training loss at later generations/epochs, yet has worse real PPL/KL—suggesting stronger overfitting or poorer generalization, consistent with the above mechanism.

Actionable implication for TADA: curriculum order is a meaningful component, but the current tail-coverage metric does not validate the “tail erosion” story here. You likely need a more discriminative tail metric (e.g., tail token frequency mass, held-out tail perplexity, rare n-gram recall, topic/semantic tail coverage) and/or harsher regimes (smaller anchors, more generations, lower-quality synthetic) to see separation. Also consider reporting confidence intervals across seeds; the observed PPL/KL gaps are small and could be within variance without error bars.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots compare curriculum order (Shuffled, Anchor-first, Synth-first) across generations on tail token coverage, real-data perplexity, and KL divergence, plus training-loss curves. Tail coverage is saturated (~1.0) with a shared dip at gen 2, providing little discriminative signal. Perplexity and KL increase with generation for all methods; Synth-first is consistently best (lowest PPL/KL) by later generations, Anchor-first worst. Training loss decreases across generations despite worse real PPL/KL, suggesting overfitting/collapse dynamics; Anchor-first shows lower training loss yet worse real metrics.
ablation_name: Curriculum Learning Order for Anchor vs. Synthetic Data

overall_plan: This research investigates iterative synthetic data generation with a focus on the TADA (tail anchor) strategy. The work proceeds in two main phases: (1) Hyperparameter tuning for learning rate (LR), testing values of 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, and 3e-3 across all strategies to identify the optimal LR based on tail token coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence metrics; and (2) An ablation study on Anchor-to-Synthetic Ratio Sensitivity, which tests 4 anchor-to-synthetic ratios (5%, 10%, 20%, 40%) while keeping TOTAL_TRAIN_SIZE=2200 constant and using the TADA strategy throughout N_GENERATIONS=5 iterative synthetic data generation rounds. The ablation study tracks the same core metrics (tail token coverage, perplexity, KL divergence) and visualizes results through generation-over-time curves, final-generation bar charts, and normalized degradation curves, culminating in a summary table identifying the best ratio per metric. Together, these experiments aim to characterize how both the learning rate and the balance between anchor and synthetic data influence model quality and tail token coverage in iterative generation pipelines.

metric_names.description: Tail token coverage measures how well the model covers tail tokens in the generated output. Higher is better.
Perplexity of the model evaluated on real data. Lower values indicate better language modeling performance.
KL divergence between the model's output distribution and the real data distribution. Lower values indicate the model is closer to the real distribution.
Training loss at the last epoch of the last generation. Lower values indicate better model fit during training.

code:
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
import numpy as np
import random
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import Counter

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)

VOCAB_SIZE = 500
SEQ_LEN = 32
N_REAL = 2000
BATCH_SIZE = 64
EPOCHS_PER_GEN = 5
N_GENERATIONS = 5
GEN_SAMPLES = 500
TAIL_FRAC = 0.10
D_MODEL = 128
N_HEAD = 4
N_LAYERS = 2
DIM_FF = 256
LR = 3e-4
TOTAL_TRAIN_SIZE = 2200

ANCHOR_RATIOS = [0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40]

rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED)
ranks = np.arange(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, dtype=np.float32)
freq_weights = 1.0 / ranks
freq_weights /= freq_weights.sum()


def make_real_corpus(n=N_REAL, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    seqs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=seq_len, p=freq_weights) + 1
        seqs.append(tokens.tolist())
    return seqs


REAL_CORPUS = make_real_corpus()

sorted_by_freq = np.argsort(freq_weights)
n_tail = int(VOCAB_SIZE * TAIL_FRAC)
TAIL_TOKENS = set((sorted_by_freq[:n_tail] + 1).tolist())
print(
    f"Tail tokens: {len(TAIL_TOKENS)} tokens (indices {min(TAIL_TOKENS)}..{max(TAIL_TOKENS)})"
)


class SeqDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, seqs):
        self.data = [torch.tensor(s, dtype=torch.long) for s in seqs]

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return self.data[i]


class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, max_len=512):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        pos = torch.arange(0, max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        div = torch.exp(
            torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * (-math.log(10000.0) / d_model)
        )
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
        self.register_buffer("pe", pe.unsqueeze(0))

    def forward(self, x):
        return x + self.pe[:, : x.size(1)]


class SmallGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(VOCAB_SIZE + 1, D_MODEL, padding_idx=0)
        self.pe = PositionalEncoding(D_MODEL)
        enc_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            D_MODEL, N_HEAD, DIM_FF, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(enc_layer, num_layers=N_LAYERS)
        self.head = nn.Linear(D_MODEL, VOCAB_SIZE + 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        sz = x.size(1)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(sz, sz, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        e = self.pe(self.embed(x))
        out = self.transformer(e, mask=mask)
        return self.head(out)


def make_model():
    return SmallGPT().to(device)


def train_model(model, seqs, epochs=EPOCHS_PER_GEN, lr=LR):
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True, drop_last=False)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    model.train()
    losses = []
    for ep in range(epochs):
        ep_loss = 0.0
        n = 0
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1), tgt.reshape(-1), ignore_index=0
            )
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * batch.size(0)
            n += batch.size(0)
        avg = ep_loss / n
        losses.append(avg)
        print(f"  epoch {ep+1}/{epochs} loss={avg:.4f}")
    return losses


@torch.no_grad()
def generate_sequences(model, n=2000, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    model.eval()
    seqs = []
    bs = 128
    for start in range(0, n, bs):
        cur = min(bs, n - start)
        tokens = torch.randint(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, (cur, 1), device=device)
        for _ in range(seq_len - 1):
            logits = model(tokens)[:, -1, :]
            logits[:, 0] = -1e9
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            nxt = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            tokens = torch.cat([tokens, nxt], dim=1)
        seqs.extend(tokens.cpu().tolist())
    return seqs


def tail_token_coverage(seqs):
    seen = set()
    for s in seqs:
        seen.update(s)
    return len(seen & TAIL_TOKENS) / len(TAIL_TOKENS)


def compute_perplexity(model, seqs, max_seqs=500):
    model.eval()
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs[:max_seqs])
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)
    total_loss = 0.0
    total_tok = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1),
                tgt.reshape(-1),
                ignore_index=0,
                reduction="sum",
            )
            total_loss += loss.item()
            total_tok += (tgt != 0).sum().item()
    return math.exp(total_loss / max(total_tok, 1))


def token_freq_vector(seqs):
    counts = Counter(t for s in seqs for t in s)
    vec = np.zeros(VOCAB_SIZE + 1)
    for t, c in counts.items():
        vec[t] = c
    s = vec.sum()
    return vec / s if s > 0 else vec


def kl_divergence(p, q, eps=1e-10):
    mask = p > 0
    return float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log((p[mask] + eps) / (q[mask] + eps))))


REAL_FREQ = token_freq_vector(REAL_CORPUS)


def anchor_tail(real_corpus, k):
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]


# experiment_data structure
experiment_data = {}

for ratio in ANCHOR_RATIOS:
    anchor_size = int(TOTAL_TRAIN_SIZE * ratio)
    synth_size = TOTAL_TRAIN_SIZE - anchor_size
    ratio_key = f"ratio_{ratio:.2f}"

    print(f"\n{'#'*70}")
    print(
        f"# Anchor Ratio: {ratio:.2f} | Anchor Size: {anchor_size} | Synth Size: {synth_size}"
    )
    print(f"{'#'*70}")

    experiment_data[ratio_key] = {
        "anchor_size": anchor_size,
        "synth_size": synth_size,
        "tail_coverage": [],
        "perplexity": [],
        "kl_divergence": [],
        "train_losses": [],
    }

    random.seed(SEED)
    np.random.seed(SEED)
    torch.manual_seed(SEED)

    model = make_model()
    print(f"[Gen 0] Train on full real corpus ({len(REAL_CORPUS)} seqs)")
    losses = train_model(model, REAL_CORPUS, lr=LR)
    experiment_data[ratio_key]["train_losses"].append(losses)

    gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES)
    ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
    ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
    kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
    experiment_data[ratio_key]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
    experiment_data[ratio_key]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
    experiment_data[ratio_key]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
    print(f"[Gen 0] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

    synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=synth_size)

    for gen in range(1, N_GENERATIONS + 1):
        print(
            f"\n[Gen {gen}] ratio={ratio:.2f} anchor={anchor_size} synth={synth_size}"
        )
        anchor = anchor_tail(REAL_CORPUS, anchor_size)
        train_seqs = anchor + synth_pool
        random.shuffle(train_seqs)
        print(
            f"  Training on {len(train_seqs)} sequences (anchor={len(anchor)}, synth={len(synth_pool)})"
        )

        model = make_model()
        losses = train_model(model, train_seqs, lr=LR)
        experiment_data[ratio_key]["train_losses"].append(losses)

        gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES)
        ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
        ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
        kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
        experiment_data[ratio_key]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
        experiment_data[ratio_key]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
        experiment_data[ratio_key]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
        print(f"[Gen {gen}] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

        synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=synth_size)

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print("\nExperiment data saved.")

# ─── Plots ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
gens = list(range(N_GENERATIONS + 1))
ratio_colors = {
    f"ratio_{r:.2f}": c

]

]

axes[0].bar(ratio_labels, final_tc, color=bar_colors)

# Plot 3: Normalized degradation
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 5))

for ratio in ANCHOR_RATIOS:

)

gens = list(range(N_GENERATIONS + 1))

    for r, c in zip(ANCHOR_RATIOS, ["red", "orange", "green", "blue"])

ratio_markers = {

# Plot 1: All metrics across generations per ratio
try:

    )

# Plot 3: Normalized degradation relative to Gen 0

# Plot 4: Training loss curves for each ratio (all generations overlaid)

for ratio in ANCHOR_RATIOS:
    rk = f"ratio_{ratio:.2f}"
    d = experiment_data[rk]
    print(
        f"{ratio:<8.2f} {d['tail_coverage'][0]:>11.4f} {d['tail_coverage'][-1]:>11.4f} {d['perplexity'][-1]:>8.2f} {d['kl_divergence'][-1]:>8.4f}"
    )
plot_plan: The experiment explores how different anchor-to-synthetic ratios affect model quality across multiple generations of synthetic data training. The key metrics tracked are tail token coverage, perplexity on real data, and KL divergence from the real token distribution. I'll create several plots: (1) all three metrics across generations for each ratio, (2) a bar chart of final-generation metrics by ratio, (3) normalized degradation curves relative to generation 0, and (4) training loss curves per ratio and generation. The data is structured as a dict with ratio keys containing lists of per-generation metric values and per-generation training losses. I'll plot training losses at a suitable interval to avoid too many figures. Each plot will be saved with a descriptive name including the dataset/experiment type.

plot_analyses.analysis: Final-generation bar summaries (Zipf synthetic text): Tail token coverage is ~saturated across ratios (≈0.98–1.0), so it is not discriminative in this regime. Perplexity on real data decreases modestly as anchor ratio increases (≈121 at 0.05 → ≈116 at 0.40), indicating better retention/generalization with more real anchors. KL divergence from real shows a much stronger dependence: ratios 0.05–0.10 have high KL (~0.087–0.091), while 0.20 drops substantially (~0.056) and 0.40 is lowest (~0.046). This suggests a threshold effect around 0.2 where distributional drift/collapse is meaningfully mitigated, even if tail coverage remains near-constant.

Across-generation trajectories (TADA, fixed total data): Tail coverage stays at 1.0 for most generations/ratios with occasional single-generation dips to ~0.98 (ratio 0.40 at gen1, ratio 0.10 at gen2, ratio 0.05 at gen5). These isolated dips look like measurement noise or a brittle coverage definition rather than systematic tail erosion. Perplexity increases with generation for all ratios, but the slope is strongly ratio-dependent: 0.05 rises from ~114.6 to ~121.5 by gen5; 0.10 to ~120; 0.20 to ~118.5; 0.40 remains nearly flat (~114.6→~116.2). KL divergence grows sharply for low ratios: 0.05 climbs to ~0.087; 0.10 is non-monotonic early (spike at gen2, dip at gen3) but ends worst at ~0.091; 0.20 peaks around gen4 then slightly improves by gen5 (~0.056); 0.40 grows slowly to ~0.046. Overall, higher anchor ratio stabilizes both perplexity and KL over iterative generations, with 0.40 clearly most robust.

Normalized degradation plots: Tail coverage normalized to gen0 is essentially 1.0 throughout, again indicating the chosen tail-coverage metric is too coarse here (ceiling effect). Relative perplexity shows compounding degradation with smaller anchors: by gen5, ~+6% (0.05), ~+4.8% (0.10), ~+3.4% (0.20), and only ~+1.4% (0.40). Relative KL amplifies differences dramatically: by gen5, ~4.9× (0.05), ~5.1× (0.10), ~3.1× (0.20), ~2.6× (0.40). This indicates distributional drift is a more sensitive collapse indicator than tail coverage in this setup, and that anchor ratio primarily controls drift accumulation rate.

Duplicate final-generation bar summaries: same pattern—tail coverage saturated; perplexity improves with higher anchor ratio; KL shows large gains when moving from 0.10→0.20 and further improvement at 0.40. The main takeaway is that the ‘effective’ anchor ratio for preventing drift appears ≥0.2 under these conditions.

Duplicate across-generation trajectories (labeled as fixed total data): confirms earlier observations—0.40 yields the flattest perplexity curve and slowest KL growth; 0.10 ends with the highest KL despite similar perplexity to 0.05, suggesting instability/non-monotonicity in KL estimation or sensitivity to which anchors are selected at that ratio.

Duplicate normalized degradation: reinforces that KL is the most discriminative metric; tail coverage is not informative due to saturation. The ordering of robustness is consistently 0.40 > 0.20 >> 0.05≈0.10, with 0.10 sometimes worse than 0.05 on KL.

Training loss curves per generation by anchor ratio: Within each ratio, later generations generally achieve lower training loss than earlier ones (gen5 lowest), indicating the model continues to fit its (increasingly synthetic) training distribution well even as evaluation metrics (PPL on real, KL to real) degrade—classic signature of model collapse/distribution shift rather than optimization failure. As anchor ratio increases, the spread between generations’ training losses narrows (especially at 0.40 where curves are tightly clustered), consistent with greater stability of the training distribution across generations. Lower anchor ratios show larger inter-generation separation (gen0 highest loss, later gens progressively lower), suggesting the synthetic data distribution is becoming easier/more concentrated over iterations (mode narrowing), which aligns with increasing KL from real.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots show an anchor-ratio ablation under TADA on a Zipf synthetic text setup. Tail token coverage is near-ceiling across conditions (not very informative), while perplexity and especially KL divergence reveal collapse/drift accumulating over generations. Higher anchor ratios (≥0.2, best at 0.4) substantially slow degradation; low ratios (0.05–0.10) show strong KL growth. Training loss decreases across generations despite worse real-data metrics, consistent with collapse via distributional simplification rather than training instability.

overall_plan: This research investigates methods to improve language model generation quality, particularly for tail coverage, perplexity on real data, and KL divergence metrics. The work began with hyperparameter tuning of the learning rate (testing values 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3) to establish optimal training configurations across all strategies. Building on this foundation, the current focus is an ablation study on 'Anchor Quality Filtering via Perplexity-Based Selection,' which examines how the difficulty/quality of anchor examples affects model performance. Five anchor selection strategies are compared: high-perplexity selection (hardest examples), low-perplexity selection (easiest examples), median-perplexity selection (typical examples), TADA (tail token heuristic), and random anchor selection. Each strategy dynamically computes per-sequence perplexities using the current model to select anchors. The experiment runs across 5 generations, tracking tail coverage, perplexity on real data, and KL divergence, with results presented in plots and summary tables. The core scientific question is whether selecting anchors based on their difficulty (as measured by model perplexity) leads to meaningfully different generation outcomes, and specifically whether the TADA tail token heuristic offers advantages over simpler perplexity-based or random selection strategies.

metric_names.description: Coverage of tail tokens in the generated output across iterative generations
Perplexity of the model evaluated on real data across iterative generations
KL divergence between the model's generated distribution and the real data distribution across iterative generations
Training loss at the final epoch of the last generation for each anchor strategy
Mean perplexity of the anchor samples at the final generation for each anchor strategy

import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
import numpy as np
import random
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import Counter

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)

VOCAB_SIZE = 500
SEQ_LEN = 32
N_REAL = 2000
ANCHOR_SIZE = 200
BATCH_SIZE = 64
EPOCHS_PER_GEN = 5
N_GENERATIONS = 5
N_SYNTH = 2000
GEN_SAMPLES = 500
TAIL_FRAC = 0.10
D_MODEL = 128
N_HEAD = 4
N_LAYERS = 2
DIM_FF = 256
LR = 3e-4

rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED)
ranks = np.arange(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, dtype=np.float32)
freq_weights = 1.0 / ranks
freq_weights /= freq_weights.sum()


def make_real_corpus(n=N_REAL, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    seqs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=seq_len, p=freq_weights) + 1
        seqs.append(tokens.tolist())
    return seqs


REAL_CORPUS = make_real_corpus()

sorted_by_freq = np.argsort(freq_weights)
n_tail = int(VOCAB_SIZE * TAIL_FRAC)
TAIL_TOKENS = set((sorted_by_freq[:n_tail] + 1).tolist())
print(
    f"Tail tokens: {len(TAIL_TOKENS)} tokens (indices {min(TAIL_TOKENS)}..{max(TAIL_TOKENS)})"
)


class SeqDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, seqs):
        self.data = [torch.tensor(s, dtype=torch.long) for s in seqs]

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return self.data[i]


class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, max_len=512):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        pos = torch.arange(0, max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        div = torch.exp(
            torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * (-math.log(10000.0) / d_model)
        )
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
        self.register_buffer("pe", pe.unsqueeze(0))

    def forward(self, x):
        return x + self.pe[:, : x.size(1)]


class SmallGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(VOCAB_SIZE + 1, D_MODEL, padding_idx=0)
        self.pe = PositionalEncoding(D_MODEL)
        enc_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            D_MODEL, N_HEAD, DIM_FF, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(enc_layer, num_layers=N_LAYERS)
        self.head = nn.Linear(D_MODEL, VOCAB_SIZE + 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        sz = x.size(1)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(sz, sz, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        e = self.pe(self.embed(x))
        out = self.transformer(e, mask=mask)
        return self.head(out)


def make_model():
    return SmallGPT().to(device)


def train_model(model, seqs, epochs=EPOCHS_PER_GEN, lr=LR):
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True, drop_last=False)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    model.train()
    losses = []
    for ep in range(epochs):
        ep_loss = 0.0
        n = 0
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1), tgt.reshape(-1), ignore_index=0
            )
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * batch.size(0)
            n += batch.size(0)
        avg = ep_loss / n
        losses.append(avg)
        print(f"  epoch {ep+1}/{epochs} loss={avg:.4f}")
    return losses


@torch.no_grad()
def generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    model.eval()
    seqs = []
    bs = 128
    for start in range(0, n, bs):
        cur = min(bs, n - start)
        tokens = torch.randint(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, (cur, 1), device=device)
        for _ in range(seq_len - 1):
            logits = model(tokens)[:, -1, :]
            logits[:, 0] = -1e9
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            nxt = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            tokens = torch.cat([tokens, nxt], dim=1)
        seqs.extend(tokens.cpu().tolist())
    return seqs


def tail_token_coverage(seqs):
    seen = set()
    for s in seqs:
        seen.update(s)
    return len(seen & TAIL_TOKENS) / len(TAIL_TOKENS)


def compute_perplexity(model, seqs, max_seqs=500):
    model.eval()
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs[:max_seqs])
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)
    total_loss = 0.0
    total_tok = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1),
                tgt.reshape(-1),
                ignore_index=0,
                reduction="sum",
            )
            total_loss += loss.item()
            total_tok += (tgt != 0).sum().item()
    return math.exp(total_loss / max(total_tok, 1))


@torch.no_grad()
def compute_per_sequence_perplexity(model, seqs):
    model.eval()
    per_seq_ppl = []
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)
    for batch in dl:
        batch = batch.to(device)
        inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
        logits = model(inp)
        B, T = tgt.shape
        logits_flat = logits.reshape(B * T, VOCAB_SIZE + 1)
        tgt_flat = tgt.reshape(B * T)
        loss_per_token = F.cross_entropy(
            logits_flat, tgt_flat, ignore_index=0, reduction="none"
        )
        loss_per_token = loss_per_token.reshape(B, T)
        mask = (tgt != 0).float()
        seq_losses = (loss_per_token * mask).sum(dim=1) / mask.sum(dim=1).clamp(min=1)
        ppls = torch.exp(seq_losses)
        per_seq_ppl.extend(ppls.cpu().tolist())
    return per_seq_ppl


def token_freq_vector(seqs):
    counts = Counter(t for s in seqs for t in s)
    vec = np.zeros(VOCAB_SIZE + 1)
    for t, c in counts.items():
        vec[t] = c
    s = vec.sum()
    return vec / s if s > 0 else vec


def kl_divergence(p, q, eps=1e-10):
    mask = p > 0
    return float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log((p[mask] + eps) / (q[mask] + eps))))


REAL_FREQ = token_freq_vector(REAL_CORPUS)


# ─── Anchor strategies ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


def anchor_random(real_corpus, model, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    idx = random.sample(range(len(real_corpus)), k)
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in idx]


def anchor_tail(real_corpus, model, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]


def anchor_high_perplexity(real_corpus, model, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    ppls = compute_per_sequence_perplexity(model, real_corpus)
    order = np.argsort(ppls)[::-1]
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]


def anchor_low_perplexity(real_corpus, model, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    ppls = compute_per_sequence_perplexity(model, real_corpus)
    order = np.argsort(ppls)
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]


def anchor_median_perplexity(real_corpus, model, k=ANCHOR_SIZE):
    ppls = compute_per_sequence_perplexity(model, real_corpus)
    order = np.argsort(ppls)
    n = len(order)
    mid = n // 2
    half_k = k // 2
    start = max(0, mid - half_k)
    end = min(n, start + k)
    selected_indices = order[start:end]
    return [real_corpus[i] for i in selected_indices]


strategies = {
    "High-PPL anchor (hardest)": anchor_high_perplexity,
    "Low-PPL anchor (easiest)": anchor_low_perplexity,
    "Median-PPL anchor (typical)": anchor_median_perplexity,
    "TADA (tail anchor)": anchor_tail,
    "Random anchor": anchor_random,
}

experiment_data = {
    name: {
        "tail_coverage": [],
        "perplexity": [],
        "kl_divergence": [],
        "train_losses": [],
        "anchor_ppl_stats": [],  # track mean ppl of selected anchors per gen
    }
    for name in strategies
}

GOLDEN_REAL_FRAC = 0.38

for strat_name, anchor_fn in strategies.items():
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"Strategy: {strat_name}")
    print("=" * 60)

    random.seed(SEED)
    np.random.seed(SEED)
    torch.manual_seed(SEED)

    model = make_model()
    print(f"[Gen 0] Train on real corpus ({len(REAL_CORPUS)} seqs)")
    losses = train_model(model, REAL_CORPUS)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["train_losses"].append(losses)

    gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES)
    ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
    ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
    kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
    experiment_data[strat_name]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
    experiment_data[strat_name]["anchor_ppl_stats"].append(None)
    print(f"[Gen 0] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

    synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH)

    for gen in range(1, N_GENERATIONS + 1):
        print(f"\n[Gen {gen}]")
        anchor = anchor_fn(REAL_CORPUS, model, ANCHOR_SIZE)
        # Track mean perplexity of selected anchors
        anchor_ppls = compute_per_sequence_perplexity(model, anchor)
        anchor_ppl_mean = float(np.mean(anchor_ppls))
        anchor_ppl_std = float(np.std(anchor_ppls))
        experiment_data[strat_name]["anchor_ppl_stats"].append(
            {"mean": anchor_ppl_mean, "std": anchor_ppl_std}
        )
        print(f"  Anchor PPL: mean={anchor_ppl_mean:.2f}, std={anchor_ppl_std:.2f}")

        train_seqs = anchor + synth_pool
        random.shuffle(train_seqs)
        print(f"  Training on {len(train_seqs)} sequences")
        model = make_model()
        losses = train_model(model, train_seqs)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["train_losses"].append(losses)

        gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES)
        ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
        ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
        kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
        experiment_data[strat_name]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
        experiment_data[strat_name]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
        print(f"[Gen {gen}] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

        synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH)

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print("\nExperiment data saved.")

# ─── Plots ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

        axes[mi].set_ylabel(mylabel)
        axes[mi].grid(True, axis="y")
        for bar, val in zip(bars, vals):
            axes[mi].text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height() + 0.001,
                f"{val:.3f}",
                ha="center",
                va="bottom",
                fontsize=8,
            )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "zipf_dataset_final_generation_bar_comparison.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: zipf_dataset_final_generation_bar_comparison.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating plot 3: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Training loss curves per strategy (flattened across epochs and generations)
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(
        1, len(strategies), figsize=(4 * len(strategies), 5), sharey=False
    )
    fig.suptitle(
        "Training Loss Curves per Strategy Across Generations\n(Zipf-distributed Token Dataset)",
        fontsize=13,
    )
    for ai, strat_name in enumerate(strategies):
        train_losses = experiment_data[strat_name]["train_losses"]
        offset = 0
        for gen_idx, gen_losses in enumerate(train_losses):
            x = list(range(offset, offset + len(gen_losses)))
            axes[ai].plot(
                x, gen_losses, marker="o", markersize=3, label=f"Gen {gen_idx}"
            )
            offset += len(gen_losses)
        axes[ai].set_title(strat_name.replace(" (", "\n("), fontsize=8)
        axes[ai].set_xlabel("Epoch (cumulative)")
        axes[ai].set_ylabel("Avg Loss")
        axes[ai].legend(fontsize=6)
        axes[ai].grid(True)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "zipf_dataset_training_loss_curves_per_strategy.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: zipf_dataset_training_loss_curves_per_strategy.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating plot 4: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 5: Delta analysis (Gen N - Gen 0) bar chart
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    fig.suptitle(
        "Delta Metrics (Gen 5 − Gen 0) by Anchor Strategy\n(Zipf-distributed Token Dataset)",
        fontsize=13,
    )
    delta_keys = ["tail_coverage", "perplexity", "kl_divergence"]
    delta_titles = [
        "ΔTail Coverage (↑ better)",
        "ΔPPL (↓ better)",
        "ΔKL Divergence (↓ better)",
    ]
    delta_ylabels = ["ΔCoverage", "ΔPPL", "ΔKL"]
    for mi, (mkey, mtitle, mylabel) in enumerate(
        zip(delta_keys, delta_titles, delta_ylabels)
    ):
        deltas = [
            experiment_data[s][mkey][-1] - experiment_data[s][mkey][0]
            for s in strategies
        ]
        bar_c = [
            "green" if (mi == 0 and d > 0) or (mi != 0 and d < 0) else "salmon"
            for d in deltas
        ]
        bars = axes[mi].bar(short_names, deltas, color=bar_c, edgecolor="black")
        axes[mi].axhline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
        axes[mi].set_title(mtitle)
        axes[mi].set_xlabel("Strategy")
        axes[mi].set_ylabel(mylabel)
        axes[mi].grid(True, axis="y")
        for bar, val in zip(bars, deltas):
            axes[mi].text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height() + (0.0005 if val >= 0 else -0.003),
                f"{val:+.3f}",
                ha="center",
                va="bottom",
                fontsize=8,
            )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "zipf_dataset_delta_metrics_bar_comparison.png"),
        dpi=150,
    )
    plt.close()
    print("Saved: zipf_dataset_delta_metrics_bar_comparison.png")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating plot 5: {e}")
    plt.close()
plot_plan: The experiment compares five anchor selection strategies for iterative model training on synthetic data, tracking tail token coverage, perplexity, KL divergence, and anchor PPL statistics across generations. I'll load the saved experiment data and create several visualizations: (1) the three main metrics across generations for all strategies, (2) anchor PPL mean and std across generations, (3) a final-generation bar chart comparison, and (4) training loss curves per strategy. I'll use the actual data keys from the experiment: `tail_coverage`, `perplexity`, `kl_divergence`, `train_losses`, and `anchor_ppl_stats`. Each strategy has metrics recorded for generations 0 through N_GENERATIONS (6 points total). The anchor PPL stats have `None` at gen 0 and dicts with `mean`/`std` for gens 1-5. Training losses are lists of per-epoch losses for each generation. I'll plot training loss curves by flattening across epochs, and use distinct colors/markers per strategy as defined in the experiment code.

plot_analyses.analysis: Tail Token Coverage at final generation is essentially saturated for all strategies (0.98–1.00). TADA is the only one at 1.00, but the metric is near-ceiling, so it is not very discriminative here. Perplexity on real data differs more: High-PPL anchors yield the lowest PPL (117.293), Random is next (119.137), Median-PPL (119.929), TADA (120.745), and Low-PPL anchors are worst (124.369). KL divergence from real shows a clearer separation: High-PPL anchors best (0.064), Random (0.074) and Median-PPL (0.078) are close behind, TADA worse (0.105), and Low-PPL worst (0.129). Overall: in this setting, selecting “hard” (high-perplexity) anchors is most effective at preserving the real distribution; TADA as implemented underperforms random/median on KL and PPL despite perfect tail coverage, suggesting the tail-coverage metric or tail definition may not align with distributional fidelity.

Across Gen0→Gen5 deltas: ΔTail coverage is identical for all strategies (−0.020), indicating the tail-coverage metric is not sensitive to anchor choice in this run (or is dominated by a global effect). ΔPPL increases for all (collapse/drift), but magnitude varies: High-PPL anchors have the smallest degradation (+2.699), Random (+4.543), Median (+5.334), TADA (+6.150), Low-PPL the largest (+9.774). ΔKL similarly: High-PPL smallest (+0.046), Random (+0.056), Median (+0.060), TADA (+0.087), Low-PPL (+0.111). This strongly supports that “hard-example anchoring” is the key driver of stability here, not tail anchoring per se; TADA is not competitive on the main drift metrics in this ablation.

Training loss curves across generations show the expected per-generation reset/shift and gradual improvement within each generation. High-PPL anchor strategy consistently has higher losses within each generation (harder training batches), while Low-PPL has lower losses (easier). Despite being harder, High-PPL anchoring correlates with better downstream fidelity metrics (lower PPL/KL on real), consistent with the idea that challenging/rare anchors regularize against collapse. TADA and Random appear similar in within-generation loss trajectories, with TADA not obviously harder than Random; this may explain why TADA doesn’t achieve the same anti-collapse benefit as High-PPL anchoring in these results.

Across generations, tail token coverage is mostly flat at 1.0 with a notable dip at Gen2 for Low-PPL (to ~0.96) and smaller dips to ~0.98 for others at Gen2 and Gen5; TADA stays at 1.0 from Gen3 onward. However, the more informative metrics (PPL and KL) show monotonic worsening with generation for all strategies, with clear ranking: High-PPL anchors consistently best (lowest PPL and KL), Low-PPL consistently worst and diverges sharply by Gen5 (PPL ~124.4, KL ~0.129). Random is generally second-best, Median close, and TADA trails Random/Median and accelerates in KL after Gen3 (ending ~0.105). Interpretation: collapse/drift is happening primarily in distributional divergence and real-data perplexity, while the chosen tail-coverage measure is near-saturated and fails to track collapse. This is a key ablation insight: anchor selection matters, but “tail” as currently operationalized is not the winner; “hardness” (high PPL under a reference model) is.

Histogram of per-sequence perplexity in the real corpus (after Gen0 training) is right-skewed with a long tail. The chosen cutoffs are P10=74.9 (easy), median=114.3 (typical), P90=182.8 (hard). This supports that High-PPL anchors are sampling from a substantially different regime (far-right tail of difficulty), which likely overlaps with rare/low-density regions. If TADA is intended to target tails, this plot suggests a simple proxy (high PPL) is already a strong tail selector in this synthetic Zipf setting; any alternative tail definition used by TADA may be misaligned with the true rarity/difficulty structure.

Mean PPL of selected anchors across generations is stable and well-separated by strategy: Low-PPL anchors ~62–64, Median ~114–116, Random ~121–128, TADA ~138–140, High-PPL ~219–224. Std dev also differs: Median has very low variance (~3–4), Low-PPL low (~9–10), High-PPL moderate (~35–37), TADA high (~46–49), Random high and increasing (~40→~51). Two implications: (1) High-PPL anchor sets are consistently hard but not extremely heterogeneous compared to Random/TADA; (2) TADA anchors are both hard-ish and highly diverse/heterogeneous, yet this does not translate into better KL/PPL outcomes—suggesting that “diversity” alone isn’t sufficient, or that TADA may include many anchors that are hard for reasons unrelated to true data tails (noise/outliers) which might not act as good anchors.

This figure appears to duplicate the previous anchor perplexity statistics but with an explicit subtitle “Zipf-distributed Token Dataset.” The same pattern holds: stable mean PPL per strategy and increasing variance for Random, high variance for TADA, moderate for High-PPL. The duplication is useful as a consistency check: anchor selection procedure is stable across generations and not drifting, so performance differences are likely due to anchor composition rather than selection instability.

Perplexity-based anchor selection ablation across generations reiterates the main story: Low-PPL anchors collapse fastest (largest PPL/KL increases), High-PPL anchors are most robust, Random and Median are intermediate, and TADA is worse than Random/Median on PPL and especially KL after Gen3. Tail coverage again is near-ceiling and not aligned with PPL/KL. This plot strengthens the conclusion that “hard-example anchoring” is the dominant beneficial component in this synthetic Zipf setup; TADA needs either a better tail definition or a stronger mechanism to ensure selected tails are the ones that matter for preserving the distribution.

Final generation metrics (Gen5) replicate the bar-chart summary: Tail coverage nearly identical (TADA at 1.0, others 0.98). PPL ranking: High-PPL best (117.293) and Low-PPL worst (124.369), with Random (119.137) and Median (119.929) beating TADA (120.745). KL ranking mirrors: High-PPL best (0.064), Random (0.074) and Median (0.078) better than TADA (0.105), Low-PPL worst (0.129). This confirms the results are consistent across the different summary visualizations.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots show that in the Zipf-token iterative synthetic training ablation, selecting high-perplexity (“hard”) real anchors most effectively reduces drift/collapse (lowest ΔPPL and ΔKL, best final PPL/KL). TADA, as implemented here, achieves perfect/near-perfect tail token coverage but underperforms random/median anchoring on the main fidelity metrics, suggesting the tail-coverage metric is saturated and/or TADA’s tail definition is misaligned with what prevents collapse in this setting. Low-perplexity (“easy”) anchors consistently perform worst.
ablation_name: Anchor Quality Filtering via Perplexity-Based Selection

overall_plan: The research involves two sequential experimental phases. First, hyperparameter tuning was conducted for the learning rate (LR), testing multiple values (1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3) across all strategies to identify the optimal LR based on tail token coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence metrics. Building on this, the current focus is an ablation study on 'Sequence Length Diversity in Anchor Samples,' which investigates how varying the length of anchor sequences affects iterative generation outcomes under the TADA (tail anchor) selection policy. Three anchor length strategies are compared: (1) fixed-length sequences (SEQ_LEN=32, the baseline), (2) uniformly sampled lengths from [16, 48], and (3) a tail-biased length distribution where sequences containing tail tokens are extended up to 48 tokens. For each strategy, the full iterative generation experiment is run across multiple generations, tracking tail token coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence. Helper functions are implemented to construct length-diverse anchor sets. All strategies are evaluated under a fixed learning rate (determined from the prior LR tuning phase), isolating the effect of anchor sequence length diversity on model behavior and tail token representation.

metric_names.description: Coverage of tail tokens in generated sequences across generations. A value of 1.0 indicates full coverage.
Perplexity of the model evaluated on real data. Lower values indicate better model fit to the real data distribution.
KL divergence between the generated and real data distributions. Lower values indicate the generated distribution is closer to the real distribution.
Training loss of the model at the last epoch of the last generation. Lower values indicate better model training performance.

code:
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
import numpy as np
import random
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import Counter

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)

VOCAB_SIZE = 500
SEQ_LEN = 32
SEQ_LEN_MIN = 16
SEQ_LEN_MAX = 48
N_REAL = 2000
ANCHOR_SIZE = 200
BATCH_SIZE = 64
EPOCHS_PER_GEN = 5
N_GENERATIONS = 5
N_SYNTH = 2000
GEN_SAMPLES = 500
TAIL_FRAC = 0.10
D_MODEL = 128
N_HEAD = 4
N_LAYERS = 2
DIM_FF = 256
LR = 3e-4

rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED)
ranks = np.arange(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, dtype=np.float32)
freq_weights = 1.0 / ranks
freq_weights /= freq_weights.sum()


def make_real_corpus(n=N_REAL, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    seqs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tokens = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=seq_len, p=freq_weights) + 1
        seqs.append(tokens.tolist())
    return seqs


REAL_CORPUS = make_real_corpus()

sorted_by_freq = np.argsort(freq_weights)
n_tail = int(VOCAB_SIZE * TAIL_FRAC)
TAIL_TOKENS = set((sorted_by_freq[:n_tail] + 1).tolist())
print(
    f"Tail tokens: {len(TAIL_TOKENS)} tokens (indices {min(TAIL_TOKENS)}..{max(TAIL_TOKENS)})"
)


class SeqDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, seqs):
        # Pad all sequences to the same length for batching
        if len(seqs) == 0:
            self.data = []
            return
        max_len = max(len(s) for s in seqs)
        self.data = []
        for s in seqs:
            t = torch.tensor(s, dtype=torch.long)
            if len(s) < max_len:
                pad = torch.zeros(max_len - len(s), dtype=torch.long)
                t = torch.cat([t, pad])
            self.data.append(t)

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return self.data[i]


class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, max_len=512):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        pos = torch.arange(0, max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        div = torch.exp(
            torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * (-math.log(10000.0) / d_model)
        )
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
        self.register_buffer("pe", pe.unsqueeze(0))

    def forward(self, x):
        return x + self.pe[:, : x.size(1)]


class SmallGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(VOCAB_SIZE + 1, D_MODEL, padding_idx=0)
        self.pe = PositionalEncoding(D_MODEL)
        enc_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            D_MODEL, N_HEAD, DIM_FF, dropout=0.1, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(enc_layer, num_layers=N_LAYERS)
        self.head = nn.Linear(D_MODEL, VOCAB_SIZE + 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        sz = x.size(1)
        mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(sz, sz, device=x.device), diagonal=1).bool()
        e = self.pe(self.embed(x))
        out = self.transformer(e, mask=mask)
        return self.head(out)


def make_model():
    return SmallGPT().to(device)


def train_model(model, seqs, epochs=EPOCHS_PER_GEN, lr=LR):
    if len(seqs) == 0:
        return []
    ds = SeqDataset(seqs)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True, drop_last=False)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    model.train()
    losses = []
    for ep in range(epochs):
        ep_loss = 0.0
        n = 0
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1), tgt.reshape(-1), ignore_index=0
            )
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * batch.size(0)
            n += batch.size(0)
        avg = ep_loss / n
        losses.append(avg)
        print(f"  epoch {ep+1}/{epochs} loss={avg:.4f}")
    return losses


@torch.no_grad()
def generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    model.eval()
    seqs = []
    bs = 128
    for start in range(0, n, bs):
        cur = min(bs, n - start)
        tokens = torch.randint(1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1, (cur, 1), device=device)
        for _ in range(seq_len - 1):
            logits = model(tokens)[:, -1, :]
            logits[:, 0] = -1e9
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
            nxt = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
            tokens = torch.cat([tokens, nxt], dim=1)
        seqs.extend(tokens.cpu().tolist())
    return seqs


def tail_token_coverage(seqs):
    seen = set()
    for s in seqs:
        seen.update(s)
    return len(seen & TAIL_TOKENS) / len(TAIL_TOKENS)


def compute_perplexity(model, seqs, max_seqs=500):
    model.eval()
    # Use fixed-length sequences from REAL_CORPUS for perplexity
    fixed_seqs = [s[:SEQ_LEN] if len(s) >= SEQ_LEN else s for s in seqs[:max_seqs]]
    # Pad to SEQ_LEN
    padded = []
    for s in fixed_seqs:
        if len(s) < SEQ_LEN:
            s = s + [0] * (SEQ_LEN - len(s))
        padded.append(s[:SEQ_LEN])
    ds = SeqDataset(padded)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=False)
    total_loss = 0.0
    total_tok = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in dl:
            batch = batch.to(device)
            inp, tgt = batch[:, :-1], batch[:, 1:]
            logits = model(inp)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE + 1),
                tgt.reshape(-1),
                ignore_index=0,
                reduction="sum",
            )
            total_loss += loss.item()
            total_tok += (tgt != 0).sum().item()
    return math.exp(total_loss / max(total_tok, 1))


def token_freq_vector(seqs):
    counts = Counter(t for s in seqs for t in s)
    vec = np.zeros(VOCAB_SIZE + 1)
    for t, c in counts.items():
        vec[t] = c
    s = vec.sum()
    return vec / s if s > 0 else vec


def kl_divergence(p, q, eps=1e-10):
    mask = p > 0
    return float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log((p[mask] + eps) / (q[mask] + eps))))


REAL_FREQ = token_freq_vector(REAL_CORPUS)


# ─── Length Strategy Functions ─────────────────────────────────────────────────


def anchor_fixed_length(real_corpus, k=ANCHOR_SIZE, seq_len=SEQ_LEN):
    """Strategy 1: Fixed length (default baseline, SEQ_LEN=32)"""
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    selected = [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]
    # Truncate or keep as is (already SEQ_LEN from corpus)
    result = []
    for s in selected:
        if len(s) >= seq_len:
            result.append(s[:seq_len])
        else:
            result.append(s + [0] * (seq_len - len(s)))
    return result


def anchor_uniform_length(
    real_corpus, k=ANCHOR_SIZE, min_len=SEQ_LEN_MIN, max_len=SEQ_LEN_MAX
):
    """Strategy 2: Uniformly sampled lengths from [16, 48]"""
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    selected = [real_corpus[i] for i in order[:k]]
    result = []
    for s in selected:
        # Sample a random length
        length = random.randint(min_len, max_len)
        if len(s) >= length:
            result.append(s[:length])
        else:
            # Extend by repeating from real corpus
            extra_needed = length - len(s)
            extra_seq = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=extra_needed, p=freq_weights) + 1
            result.append(s + extra_seq.tolist())
    return result


def anchor_tail_biased_length(
    real_corpus, k=ANCHOR_SIZE, short_len=SEQ_LEN, long_len=SEQ_LEN_MAX
):
    """Strategy 3: Tail-biased length - sequences with tail tokens get longer context"""
    scores = [sum(1 for t in seq if t in TAIL_TOKENS) for seq in real_corpus]
    order = np.argsort(scores)[::-1]
    selected_indices = order[:k]
    selected_scores = [scores[i] for i in selected_indices]
    max_score = max(selected_scores) if max(selected_scores) > 0 else 1

    result = []
    for idx, score in zip(selected_indices, selected_scores):
        s = real_corpus[idx]
        # Sequences with more tail tokens get longer length
        # Linear interpolation: score=0 -> short_len, score=max -> long_len
        frac = score / max_score
        target_len = int(short_len + frac * (long_len - short_len))
        target_len = max(short_len, min(long_len, target_len))

        if len(s) >= target_len:
            result.append(s[:target_len])
        else:
            # Extend with real corpus tokens
            extra_needed = target_len - len(s)
            extra_seq = rng.choice(VOCAB_SIZE, size=extra_needed, p=freq_weights) + 1
            result.append(s + extra_seq.tolist())
    return result


# ─── Ablation Strategies ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

length_strategies = {
    "fixed_length": {
        "name": "Fixed Length (SEQ_LEN=32)",
        "anchor_fn": anchor_fixed_length,
        "gen_len": SEQ_LEN,
        "color": "green",
        "ls": "-",
    },
    "uniform_length": {
        "name": "Uniform Length [16, 48]",
        "anchor_fn": anchor_uniform_length,
        "gen_len": SEQ_LEN,
        "color": "blue",
        "ls": "--",
    },
    "tail_biased_length": {
        "name": "Tail-Biased Length",
        "anchor_fn": anchor_tail_biased_length,
        "gen_len": SEQ_LEN,
        "color": "orange",
        "ls": "-.",
    },
}

# ─── experiment_data structure ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
experiment_data = {
    strat_key: {
        "tail_coverage": [],
        "perplexity": [],
        "kl_divergence": [],
        "train_losses": [],
    }
    for strat_key in length_strategies
}

# ─── Main Experiment Loop ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
for strat_key, strat_info in length_strategies.items():
    strat_name = strat_info["name"]
    anchor_fn = strat_info["anchor_fn"]
    gen_len = strat_info["gen_len"]

    print(f"\n{'='*70}")
    print(f"Length Strategy: {strat_name}")
    print("=" * 70)

    random.seed(SEED)
    np.random.seed(SEED)
    torch.manual_seed(SEED)

    model = make_model()
    print(f"[Gen 0] Train on real corpus ({len(REAL_CORPUS)} seqs)")
    losses = train_model(model, REAL_CORPUS, lr=LR)
    experiment_data[strat_key]["train_losses"].append(losses)

    gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES, seq_len=gen_len)
    ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
    ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
    kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
    experiment_data[strat_key]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
    experiment_data[strat_key]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
    experiment_data[strat_key]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
    print(f"[Gen 0] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

    synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, seq_len=gen_len)

    for gen in range(1, N_GENERATIONS + 1):
        print(f"\n[Gen {gen}]")
        anchor = anchor_fn(REAL_CORPUS, ANCHOR_SIZE)
        train_seqs = anchor + synth_pool
        random.shuffle(train_seqs)
        print(f"  Training on {len(train_seqs)} sequences")
        model = make_model()
        losses = train_model(model, train_seqs, lr=LR)
        experiment_data[strat_key]["train_losses"].append(losses)

        gen_seqs = generate_sequences(model, n=GEN_SAMPLES, seq_len=gen_len)
        ttc = tail_token_coverage(gen_seqs)
        ppl = compute_perplexity(model, REAL_CORPUS)
        kl = kl_divergence(REAL_FREQ, token_freq_vector(gen_seqs))
        experiment_data[strat_key]["tail_coverage"].append(ttc)
        experiment_data[strat_key]["perplexity"].append(ppl)
        experiment_data[strat_key]["kl_divergence"].append(kl)
        print(f"[Gen {gen}] TailCoverage={ttc:.4f} PPL={ppl:.2f} KL={kl:.4f}")

        synth_pool = generate_sequences(model, n=N_SYNTH, seq_len=gen_len)

np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print("\nExperiment data saved.")

# ─── Plotting ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
gens = list(range(N_GENERATIONS + 1))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
fig.suptitle("Sequence Length Diversity in Anchor Samples: Ablation Study", fontsize=13)

for strat_key, strat_info in length_strategies.items():

axes[1].set_xlabel("Generation")

axes[2].set_xlabel("Generation")
axes[2].set_ylabel("KL")

fig.suptitle("Sequence Length Diversity: Per-Metric Detailed View", fontsize=13)

metric_info = [
    ("tail_coverage", "Tail Token Coverage (↑)", "Coverage"),

for mi, (metric, title, ylabel) in enumerate(metric_info):

plot_plan: The experiment compares three anchor length strategies (fixed, uniform, tail-biased) across 5 generations of synthetic data training. The data contains tail_coverage, perplexity, kl_divergence, and train_losses for each strategy across generations 0-5. I'll create four types of plots: (1) a combined multi-metric line plot across generations, (2) per-metric detailed views, (3) a final generation bar chart comparison, and (4) training loss curves per strategy. Each strategy is color-coded (green=fixed, blue=uniform, orange=tail-biased) matching the experiment code. I'll load the data carefully using allow_pickle=True and access the nested dictionary structure directly. Each plot will be in its own try-except block with descriptive filenames including dataset/strategy context.

plot_analyses.analysis: Final-generation (Gen 5) bar comparison across anchor length strategies shows: Tail token coverage is identical at 1.000 for Fixed length (SEQ_LEN=32), Uniform length [16,48], and Tail-biased length, implying this metric is saturated / not discriminative in this setup. Perplexity on real data differs modestly: Fixed 120.758, Uniform 120.352, Tail-biased 119.672 (best). KL divergence from real differs more: Fixed 0.092 (worst), Uniform 0.075, Tail-biased 0.061 (best). This indicates sequence-length diversity—especially tail-biased length—improves distributional fidelity and generalization to real data, even when tail coverage is already maxed out.

Across generations (0→5) with per-metric detailed lines: Tail token coverage remains ~1.0 for all strategies except a shared dip to ~0.98 at Gen 2, then returns to 1.0; since all curves overlap, length strategy is not affecting this metric and the Gen 2 dip likely comes from a shared pipeline event (e.g., sampling temperature, dataset size, or evaluation artifact). Perplexity increases monotonically with generation for all strategies (collapse signal), but Tail-biased length consistently yields lower PPL from Gen 3 onward, with widening gap by Gen 5. KL divergence increases with generation; Tail-biased length grows slower and plateaus around ~0.061 at Gen 4–5, while Fixed and Uniform continue increasing (Fixed to ~0.092, Uniform to ~0.075). The key insight is that length strategy affects distributional drift (KL) more strongly than tail coverage; tail-biased length acts as a regularizer against drift in later generations.

All-metrics-across-generations panel (same content as prior but compact) reinforces: (i) tail coverage saturation/overlap; (ii) PPL drift upward for all, with Tail-biased best; (iii) KL drift upward, with Tail-biased showing a clear late-generation advantage and apparent stabilization after Gen 4. Notably, Fixed length exhibits a sharper KL jump from Gen 3→4 and Gen 4→5 than the others, suggesting fixed-length anchors may underrepresent structural variability needed to constrain the model’s distribution across iterative self-training.

Per-metric detailed view (duplicate of the earlier detailed lines) again highlights that the meaningful separation is in KL and PPL, not tail coverage. The consistent ordering at later generations is Tail-biased < Uniform < Fixed for both PPL and KL, suggesting that increasing anchor length diversity is beneficial and that skewing toward longer/rarer lengths yields additional gains.

Gen0→Gen5 delta bars: Δ tail coverage is +0.000 for all strategies (again saturated). Δ perplexity: Fixed +6.163, Uniform +5.758, Tail-biased +5.078 (smallest degradation). Δ KL: Fixed +0.074, Uniform +0.057, Tail-biased +0.043 (smallest drift). This is a clean ablation result: tail-biased length reduces the rate of collapse/drift per generation relative to fixed-length anchors, with uniform-length as an intermediate improvement.

Sequence length diversity ablation study lines (same as above) confirm robustness of the pattern: Tail-biased length yields the lowest PPL and KL at later generations, while tail token coverage remains non-informative. The divergence between strategies becomes most visible after Gen 3, suggesting early generations are dominated by other factors (e.g., synthetic data still close to real), and length strategy matters more once drift accumulates.

Training loss curves by length strategy (per strategy panels with multiple generations): Within each generation, loss decreases quickly from epoch 0→1 and then plateaus, indicating optimization is stable and comparable across strategies. Across generations, later generations start at lower initial loss and end at lower final loss (e.g., Gen 5 lowest), which can happen if the model is training on progressively easier/more self-similar synthetic data—consistent with collapse (training becomes easier while real-data PPL worsens). Differences between strategies are subtle in training loss, implying the observed PPL/KL improvements for tail-biased length are not simply due to better training convergence but due to better anchoring of the learned distribution.

Training loss curves across generations (combined view) reiterate the same phenomenon: training loss monotonically decreases with generation for all strategies, while evaluation metrics (PPL/KL) worsen—classic signature of self-training collapse / distribution narrowing. Tail-biased length does not markedly change training loss, but it improves external fidelity metrics, supporting the claim that anchor curation affects what is preserved rather than how well the model fits its training mixture.

Final generation metrics bar chart (duplicate of the earlier final-generation comparison) again shows: identical tail coverage (1.000), modest PPL improvement for tail-biased (119.672 vs 120.352 vs 120.758), and substantial KL improvement (0.061 vs 0.075 vs 0.092). The effect size is larger on KL than PPL, suggesting KL is the more sensitive collapse metric for this ablation.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots compare anchor sequence-length strategies (fixed, uniform range, tail-biased) over 6 generations. Tail token coverage saturates at ~1.0 for all strategies (non-discriminative), with a shared Gen-2 dip. Perplexity on real data and KL divergence from real worsen with generation (collapse), but tail-biased length consistently reduces degradation, especially in later generations, and appears to stabilize KL by Gen 4–5. Training loss decreases across generations despite worse real-data metrics, consistent with self-training collapse; length strategy barely changes training loss, implying improvements come from better distribution anchoring rather than optimization.
ablation_name: Sequence Length Diversity in Anchor Samples

overall_plan: This research investigates strategies to combat distributional collapse in language model training, with a focus on maintaining tail token coverage across generations. The work began with hyperparameter tuning of the learning rate (testing values 1e-4, 3e-4, 1e-3, 3e-3 across all strategies) to identify optimal training configurations based on tail coverage, perplexity, and KL divergence metrics. Building on this foundation, the current phase introduces an ablation study called 'Anchor Token Diversity Boosting via Rare Token Injection,' which investigates whether artificially amplifying tail token exposure in anchor sequences can slow distributional collapse. Three variants of anchor sequence construction are compared: (1) no tail token injection (baseline using standard TADA-style tail-based anchor selection from the real corpus), (2) injecting 1 tail token per anchor sequence by randomly replacing positions with rare/tail tokens, and (3) injecting 3 tail tokens per anchor sequence. The core scientific question is whether deliberately enriching training anchor sequences with rare tokens helps preserve distributional diversity over multiple generations (5 generations tracked). All variants are evaluated using tail coverage, KL divergence, and perplexity metrics, with results saved and visualized for comparison. The implementation follows a structured ablation code framework with proper seeding to ensure reproducibility.

KL divergence from the real data distribution at generation 0 (initial) across dataset variants.
